proposals.title: Reversing the Causal Flow: Investigating ICT Acceptance and Use as Precursors to Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (TPACK) Development in Science Teachers
proposals.motivation: The TPACK framework is widely used to assess teacher readiness for technology integration, often positioning TPACK as a prerequisite for effective ICT use. However, this assumption may overlook a dynamic, practice-driven developmental pathway. Given the persistent barriers to technology-integrated instruction identified in Tsai's prior work, understanding if and how actual ICT engagement can build TPACK is crucial for designing more effective teacher professional development and support systems in the pre-2026 context of increasing digital learning environments.
proposals.hypothesis: High levels of Information and Communication Technology (ICT) acceptance and sustained, pedagogically relevant use in science teaching are significant predictors of subsequent growth in teachers' integrated Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (TPACK), reversing the commonly assumed causal direction.
proposals.method: This study will employ a longitudinal, mixed-methods design with in-service science teachers. Quantitative data will be collected via validated scales measuring ICT acceptance (UTAUT model), frequency/quality of ICT use, and TPACK at three time points over one academic year. Cross-lagged panel modeling (CLPM) will be used to test the directional relationships between constructs. Qualitative data from stimulated-recall interviews and lesson artifacts will provide rich descriptions of how specific ICT practices influence teachers' epistemic and pedagogical reasoning, informing the quantitative model.
proposals.contribution: This research will challenge and refine the theoretical understanding of TPACK development by empirically testing an alternative, practice-to-knowledge causal pathway. It will provide actionable insights for teacher educators, suggesting that lowering initial barriers to ICT experimentation and use may be a viable strategy for fostering deeper integrative knowledge, thereby addressing the practical challenges of technology integration highlighted in prior research.
proposals.novelty: While most pre-2026 research treats TPACK as an independent variable or outcome of direct training, this proposal explicitly tests ICT acceptance and use as independent variables predicting TPACK growth, a reversed causal model that is theoretically posited but rarely tested with longitudinal data in authentic teaching contexts.

proposals.title: Beyond Vocational Contexts: Validating the TPACK-TAM Integration Model for University-Level EFL Teachers in China
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, research on technology integration in Chinese EFL teaching, particularly using the integrated Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (TPACK) and Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) framework, has been predominantly conducted in vocational college settings. This creates a significant gap in understanding whether the established relationships between teachers' knowledge, beliefs, and behavioral intentions are generalizable to university-level contexts, where educational goals, student profiles, and institutional resources differ substantially. Validating this model across diverse educational tiers is crucial for developing effective, context-sensitive professional development and technology implementation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: The integrated TPACK-TAM model, which posits that TPACK constructs (like Technological Pedagogical Knowledge) directly influence perceived usefulness and ease of use (TAM), which in turn predict behavioral intention to use technology, will demonstrate significantly different structural relationships and predictive strengths for university-level EFL teachers compared to the established vocational college teacher profile.
proposals.method: This study will employ a cross-sectional survey design with structural equation modeling (SEM) as the primary analytical technique. A validated questionnaire measuring TPACK constructs (TK, PK, CK, TPK, TCK, TPCK), TAM constructs (Perceived Usefulness, Perceived Ease of Use, Behavioral Intention), and contextual barriers will be administered to a stratified random sample of 400+ EFL teachers from comprehensive and technical universities across China. Using Mplus or AMOS software, a multi-group confirmatory factor analysis (MGCFA) will first establish measurement invariance. Subsequently, a multi-group SEM will be conducted to test for significant differences in the structural path coefficients (e.g., TPCK -> PU) between the new university sample and a pre-existing vocational college dataset, controlling for teaching experience and institutional support.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide empirical evidence on the boundary conditions of the TPACK-TAM model, clarifying its applicability beyond vocational education. It will yield a refined, context-aware model for university EFL settings, directly informing the design of targeted teacher training programs that address the specific epistemic and technological barriers faced by this population. The findings will contribute to the literature on teacher beliefs and technology integration by highlighting how institutional ecology shapes the knowledge-acceptance-behavior nexus.
proposals.novelty: While the TPACK-TAM integration is known, its systematic validation and comparison across distinct levels of China's higher education system (vocational vs. university) for EFL teachers is novel. This moves beyond documenting barriers to explicitly testing the structural stability of a dominant theoretical model across an understudied contextual dimension.

proposals.title: Beyond Perceived Usefulness and Ease: Testing the Incremental Validity of Self-Efficacy and Anxiety in Teachers' Technology Acceptance for Science Instruction
proposals.motivation: The Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) is foundational for understanding technology integration in education, but its core constructs (Perceived Usefulness, Perceived Ease of Use, and Attitude) may not fully capture the psychological and epistemic barriers teachers face, especially in complex domains like science. Pre-2026 research by Tsai and others highlights that teacher beliefs, self-efficacy, and anxiety are critical yet often external factors in technology adoption. This study directly tests whether integrating these constructs into TAM significantly improves its explanatory power for predicting actual usage intentions in science classrooms.
proposals.hypothesis: Adding the constructs of technology-integrated science teaching self-efficacy and technology anxiety to the core TAM will provide statistically significant incremental validity in predicting teachers' behavioral intention to use mobile and augmented reality tools for science instruction, beyond the variance explained by Perceived Usefulness, Perceived Ease of Use, and Attitude.
proposals.method: The study will employ a cross-sectional survey design with in-service science teachers. Validated scales for TAM core constructs, technology-integrated science teaching self-efficacy, and technology anxiety will be used. Data analysis will involve a two-step structural equation modeling (SEM) approach using Mplus or SmartPLS. First, a baseline model with only TAM core constructs will be tested. Second, the expanded model incorporating self-efficacy (as an antecedent to PU and PEU) and anxiety (as an antecedent to PEU and a direct barrier to Attitude) will be fitted. Nested model comparison via chi-square difference tests and changes in explained variance (R²) for Behavioral Intention will determine incremental validity.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide empirical evidence on the sufficiency of TAM's parsimonious core for teacher-focused educational technology research. It will contribute a validated, context-specific expansion of TAM for science education, directly informing the design of teacher professional development by pinpointing whether interventions should target utilitarian perceptions (PU/PEU) or affective-cognitive barriers (self-efficacy, anxiety) for more effective technology integration.
proposals.novelty: While TAM extensions are common, this proposal uniquely integrates Tsai's pre-2026 focus on teacher epistemic barriers and self-efficacy within a rigorous test of incremental validity, moving beyond simple model expansion to formally question the sufficiency of TAM's core in the specific, high-stakes context of science teaching.

proposals.title: Unpacking the Epistemic Core: A Qualitative Study of How TPACK Components Shape Teachers' Perceptions of ICT Usefulness and Ease of Use
proposals.motivation: Despite widespread promotion of the Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (TPACK) framework, the specific mechanisms by which its components influence teachers' core acceptance beliefs—perceived usefulness and ease of use—remain a 'black box.' Prior quantitative work by Tsai and others shows teachers' beliefs and perceived barriers are critical to technology integration, but a nuanced, qualitative understanding is lacking. This gap hinders the design of targeted professional development to move teachers beyond surface-level adoption to meaningful, epistemically sound ICT use in science education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that teachers' Technological Knowledge (TK) primarily shapes perceived ease of use, while the integrative knowledge components—Pedagogical Content Knowledge (PCK) and Technological Pedagogical Knowledge (TPK)—are more influential in shaping perceived usefulness, as they connect technology to core epistemic goals of teaching and learning.
proposals.method: We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 25-30 in-service science teachers, selected for variance in ICT experience. The interview protocol will use scenario-based prompts and critical incident technique to elicit rich descriptions of planning and implementing ICT lessons. A thematic analysis, guided by a priori codes from the TPACK framework and the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM), will be performed using NVivo software to identify causal pathways and interactions between specific TPACK domains and acceptance beliefs.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a fine-grained, explanatory model of the TPACK-TAM relationship, moving beyond correlation to mechanism. It will contribute to the literature on teachers' epistemic beliefs about technology by revealing how different knowledge types act as cognitive filters for evaluating ICT. The findings will directly inform the design of more effective, component-specific teacher development programs to overcome integration barriers.
proposals.novelty: While TPACK and TAM have been studied separately, this work uniquely employs a qualitative, mechanism-focused approach to dissect *how* specific TPACK components differentially drive the two key acceptance constructs, an underexplored linkage in pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: A Cross-Regional and Cross-Disciplinary Investigation of In-Service Teachers' Technology Integration: Validating and Extending the TPACK-TAM Model in the Chinese Context
proposals.motivation: Prior research by Tsai and others has identified subject-specific and contextual barriers to technology integration, primarily in science and general instruction. However, the generalizability of the influential TPACK (Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge) and TAM (Technology Acceptance Model) integration framework across diverse academic disciplines and the varied socio-economic and infrastructural landscapes within China remains underexplored. This gap limits the development of effective, scalable professional development strategies for nationwide digital education initiatives.
proposals.hypothesis: The structural relationships within the integrated TPACK-TAM model, particularly the paths from TPACK constructs to perceived usefulness and ease of use, will vary significantly across different school subjects (e.g., humanities, arts, vocational training) and across provinces with differing educational resource levels.
proposals.method: This study will employ a cross-sectional survey design, replicating and extending Tsai's prior instrumentation. A stratified random sample of in-service K-12 teachers will be recruited from multiple provinces (e.g., Zhejiang, Henan, Gansu) representing high, medium, and low resource tiers. The survey will measure TPACK domains, TAM constructs (perceived usefulness, ease of use, behavioral intention), and contextual barriers. Data analysis will use Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with multi-group analysis in software like Mplus or AMOS (pre-2026 standards) to test for measurement invariance and compare the strength of model pathways across subject and regional groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence on the boundary conditions of the TPACK-TAM model, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all understanding. It will yield a nuanced map of how discipline-specific pedagogical needs and regional resource disparities shape teachers' epistemic and practical barriers to technology use, informing more targeted policy and teacher training programs in China's heterogeneous educational system.
proposals.novelty: While TPACK-TAM studies exist, few simultaneously dissect the model's validity across both disciplinary content (beyond STEM) and sub-national regional contexts within a single, large-scale education system, offering a unique test of context-dependency in teachers' technology-related beliefs.

proposals.title: Beyond Individual Competence: The Moderating Role of Institutional Support in the Relationship Between Teacher TPACK and ICT Integration Intention
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, research on technology integration in education, including Dr. Tsai's work on barriers, consistently identifies Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (TPACK) as a key predictor of teachers' behavioral intention to use ICT. However, the translation of this knowledge into practice is often hindered by contextual factors. The COVID-19 pandemic's shift to massive distance education starkly revealed how institutional infrastructure and policy can enable or constrain even highly knowledgeable teachers, making it critical to model these systemic influences explicitly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the positive relationship between in-service teachers' TPACK and their behavioral intention to integrate ICT is significantly moderated by perceived institutional support, encompassing both tangible resources (e.g., technology access, technical assistance) and intangible policies (e.g., administrative encouragement, professional development opportunities).
proposals.method: We will employ a quantitative survey design with established scales for TPACK, ICT behavioral intention, and a newly validated scale for perceived institutional support (covering resources and policies). Data will be collected from a stratified sample of K-12 in-service science teachers. Analysis will use hierarchical multiple regression and PROCESS macro (Hayes, 2018) to test for moderation effects, specifically examining the interaction term between TPACK and institutional support scores on intention.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a more nuanced, systems-level understanding of technology integration by quantifying how school context interacts with teacher competence. It will offer actionable insights for administrators and policymakers on where to target support to maximize the impact of TPACK development. The findings will extend barrier-focused models by empirically testing institutional factors as moderators rather than just additive barriers.
proposals.novelty: While existing work lists institutional factors as barriers, this study innovatively positions them as statistical moderators within the established TPACK-intention framework, testing a specific interaction effect that is often assumed but not rigorously modeled in pre-2026 literature on teacher technology use.

proposals.title: Capturing the Dynamics of Technology Integration: A Real-Time Experience Sampling Study of In-Service Teachers' TPACK and ICT Use Intentions
proposals.motivation: Prior research on teachers' Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (TPACK) and barriers to technology integration (pre-2026) relies heavily on retrospective self-reports, which are prone to recall bias and cannot capture the dynamic, context-dependent nature of teachers' perceptions and decisions during actual teaching. Understanding these momentary cognitive and motivational states is crucial for designing effective, just-in-time professional development and support systems, especially in the wake of the massive shift to online and blended learning environments.
proposals.hypothesis: Teachers' momentary perceptions of their TPACK and their immediate intentions to use ICT in teaching are significantly influenced by concurrent contextual factors (e.g., subject being taught, lesson phase, student engagement) and fluctuate more than stable, global TPACK self-assessments would predict.
proposals.method: We will employ the Experience Sampling Method (ESM) via a dedicated mobile application. In-service science teachers (N≈50) will be prompted 4-5 times per teaching day over a two-week period. Each prompt will deliver a brief survey measuring state-level TPACK perceptions (e.g., "Right now, I feel confident combining technology with my science content for this lesson"), ICT use intention, and contextual variables. Data will be analyzed using multilevel modeling (MLM) to partition within- and between-teacher variance and to model the effects of situational factors on the dependent variables.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, ecologically valid dataset on the real-time dynamics of TPACK and technology integration intentions. It will move the field beyond static barrier models by identifying specific, actionable teaching contexts where support is most needed. The methodology will establish a new approach for studying teacher cognition in situ.
proposals.novelty: While ESM is established in psychology, its application to study the moment-to-moment fluctuations of TPACK is novel. This approach directly addresses the limitation of cross-sectional surveys dominant in pre-2026 TPACK and barrier research by capturing the situated and temporal nature of teachers' technology integration decisions.

proposals.title: Deconstructing Teacher Cognition: A Cognitive Task Analysis of TPACK-in-Action During ICT-Integrated Science Lesson Planning
proposals.motivation: Despite the widespread adoption of the Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (TPACK) framework, the in-situ cognitive processes teachers employ when planning technology-integrated lessons remain a 'black box.' This gap limits the design of effective professional development, as evidenced by persistent teacher-reported barriers to technology integration. A fine-grained understanding of these cognitive processes is crucial for moving beyond theoretical models to actionable instructional support.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Cognitive Task Analysis (CTA) will reveal distinct, sequential cognitive sub-tasks and critical decision points within the TPACK framework that are not captured by self-report surveys, and that expert and novice teachers will demonstrate qualitatively different cognitive pathways and heuristics during ICT-integrated lesson planning.
proposals.method: This study will employ a multi-method CTA approach with in-service science teachers. First, a Knowledge Audit interview will map the declarative and procedural knowledge required for TPACK. Second, a Critical Decision Method will be used in a simulated lesson-planning task to elicit retrospective accounts of non-routine challenges and decisions. Third, concurrent think-aloud protocols will be recorded during planning with screen capture. Data will be analyzed using established CTA coding schemes (e.g., for goal decomposition, situation assessment, and decision strategies) and qualitative thematic analysis to construct a cognitive process model of TPACK application.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a granular, empirically grounded cognitive model of TPACK application, directly linking theoretical knowledge domains to observable planning behaviors. It will contribute a novel methodological approach (CTA) to the study of teacher cognition and technology integration, offering a template for diagnosing specific cognitive barriers. The findings will inform the design of targeted cognitive scaffolds and training interventions for teacher education programs.
proposals.novelty: While TPACK is well-studied via surveys and outcome measures, this study innovatively applies human-factors engineering methods (CTA) to open the cognitive 'black box' of the framework's real-time application. It shifts the focus from what teachers *believe* or *have* to *how they think* when synthesizing technology, pedagogy, and content.

proposals.title: Co-designing for Contextualized TPACK: A Design-Based Research Study on Iterative Development of Teacher Professional Learning Activities
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Tsai's own work, consistently identifies persistent barriers to effective technology integration in science teaching, often rooted in teachers' beliefs and epistemic challenges. Traditional professional development frequently fails to address the complex, situated nature of Pedagogical Content Knowledge (PCK) and its integration with technology (TPACK), leading to interventions that measure outcomes but do not evolve with teacher needs.
proposals.hypothesis: Iteratively co-designing TPACK development activities with in-service science teachers through Design-Based Research (DBR) will produce more contextually relevant and sustainable learning designs, which in turn will lead to measurable shifts in teachers' epistemic beliefs about technology and reduced perceived barriers to integration.
proposals.method: This study employs a multi-cycle Design-Based Research (DBR) methodology. We will collaborate with 15-20 secondary science teachers over three iterative design cycles (each ~8 weeks). Each cycle involves: 1) Co-design workshops to prototype technology-integrated lesson activities (e.g., using AR for molecular models, sensors for data collection), 2) Implementation of prototypes in teachers' classrooms, 3) Data collection via structured TPACK surveys, teacher belief interviews, classroom video analysis, and student cognitive load/engagement measures (extending Tsai's prior methods), and 4) Collaborative analysis and redesign. Data analysis will integrate qualitative thematic analysis of co-design sessions and interviews with quantitative pre-post measures of TPACK and epistemic beliefs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, co-designed suite of TPACK development activities grounded in authentic classroom practice. It will advance theoretical understanding of how DBR can address the 'wicked problem' of teacher belief change and technology integration. The study will produce practical design principles for creating adaptive, context-sensitive professional learning that moves beyond one-size-fits-all models.
proposals.novelty: While DBR is established in learning sciences, its application to the *iterative co-design of the teacher professional development process itself*—rather than just student-facing curricula—is novel. This approach treats teachers' evolving TPACK and beliefs as the primary design target, directly addressing the epistemic and contextual barriers identified in prior work.

proposals.title: Assessing the Impact of National ICT-in-Education Policies: A Large-Scale Structural Equation Modeling Study of Teacher TPACK and Technology Acceptance
proposals.motivation: Many nations have invested heavily in ICT-in-education policies, but their large-scale impact on the crucial teacher-level factors of Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (TPACK) and technology acceptance remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 research, including Tsai's work on barriers to technology-integrated instruction, highlights a persistent gap between policy implementation and classroom practice, necessitating a systematic, evidence-based evaluation of these national initiatives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effectiveness of national ICT-in-education policies on classroom integration is mediated by a sequential pathway, where policy support directly influences teachers' perceived barriers and institutional support, which in turn shapes their TPACK development and ultimately their behavioral intention to use technology, as modeled by an extended Technology Acceptance Model (TAM).
proposals.method: We will conduct a nationwide survey of in-service science teachers, employing stratified random sampling to ensure representativeness. The analysis will use Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with a Maximum Likelihood estimator to test the hypothesized mediation model. Specific latent constructs will include policy perception, TPACK (measured via a validated survey), perceived barriers, and TAM variables (perceived usefulness, ease of use). Model fit will be assessed using CFI, TLI, RMSEA, and SRMR indices.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical, large-scale evidence on the mechanisms linking macro-level policy to micro-level teacher cognition and behavior. It will contribute a validated assessment model for policy impact that can be adapted across contexts and identify which policy-supported factors (e.g., reducing specific barriers) most effectively promote TPACK and acceptance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines TPACK or barriers in isolation, this study uniquely integrates these constructs within a policy evaluation framework, scaling up Tsai's previous institutional-level barrier analysis to a national systems level to directly inform educational governance and policy refinement.

proposals.title: Inferring Latent TPACK Profiles: A Bayesian Network Model of Observed ICT Integration Patterns in Science Classrooms
proposals.motivation: Effective teacher professional development in technology integration hinges on accurate assessment of Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (TPACK). Pre-2026, TPACK is primarily measured via self-report surveys, which are susceptible to bias and do not capture enacted knowledge in practice. An inference model based on observable classroom ICT use offers a more objective, scalable, and diagnostic tool for understanding teachers' authentic capabilities and barriers.
proposals.hypothesis: A teacher's observable patterns of ICT tool selection, pedagogical application, and content alignment in lesson artifacts and observational data are reliable indicators of their latent, interdependent TPACK constructs (TK, PK, CK, TPK, TCK, PCK, TPACK).
proposals.method: We will collect multi-modal data from secondary science teachers, including lesson plans, classroom video recordings, and digital artifacts. Using pre-2026 computer vision and natural language processing techniques (e.g., OpenPose for interaction analysis, BERT for instructional text classification), we will extract features quantifying ICT use frequency, pedagogical purpose, and content relevance. A Bayesian Network will be constructed to model the probabilistic relationships between these observed features and the latent TPACK variables, with parameters learned using expectation-maximization algorithms. Model inference will be validated against a robust criterion measure combining stimulated recall interviews and expert ratings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, evidence-based methodology for TPACK assessment that moves beyond self-report. It will yield a computational model that can diagnose specific TPACK component strengths and weaknesses from teaching traces, directly informing personalized teacher support. The research will also advance the theoretical understanding of how TPACK manifests in observable instructional practice.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies barriers to ICT use, this study inverts the problem to infer the underlying knowledge causing those usage patterns. It applies probabilistic graphical modeling, common in educational data mining for student learning, to the underexplored domain of teacher cognition and practice.

proposals.title: Beyond Distraction: Investigating Moderate Online Gaming as a Protective Factor for Undergraduate Students' Social Self-Efficacy and Cognitive Resilience in Digital Learning Environments
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on technology in education, including Dr. Tsai's work, predominantly frames digital activities like online gaming as potential barriers to learning, focusing on cognitive load and distraction. However, emerging pre-2026 literature in psychology suggests certain digital leisure activities may foster problem-solving and social capital. This study addresses a critical gap by empirically testing if moderate gaming, often viewed as a barrier, can paradoxically confer benefits in academic contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that undergraduate students who engage in moderate (non-addictive) levels of online cooperative gaming will demonstrate higher levels of perceived social support and cognitive resilience, which will positively correlate with their science learning self-efficacy when facing challenging digital learning tasks.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods design. First, a survey will measure gaming habits (using the Internet Gaming Disorder Scale-Short Form), science learning self-efficacy, perceived social support, and cognitive resilience (using validated scales). We will then conduct a quasi-experiment: students will be grouped based on gaming profiles (non-gamers, moderate gamers, high-frequency gamers) and engage with an Augmented Reality (AR) science simulation designed to induce manageable cognitive load. Their in-task behavior patterns (e.g., persistence, help-seeking, collaboration) will be logged and analyzed using sequence mining algorithms (e.g., Lag Sequential Analysis). Finally, focus groups will explore the epistemic beliefs students hold about gaming and learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a nuanced, evidence-based perspective on student digital behaviors, moving beyond a deficit model. It will provide educators with insights into how to leverage students' existing digital literacies and social networks to foster resilience. The findings will inform the design of more holistic technology-integrated instruction that acknowledges the complex role of leisure technology in students' lives.
proposals.novelty: This study is novel in directly inverting the dominant 'barrier' framework from Dr. Tsai's prior work to investigate gaming as a potential protective factor. It uniquely integrates concepts of social benefit and cognitive resilience with established measures of learning self-efficacy and epistemic beliefs within a science education context.

proposals.title: Designing for Productive Friction: An Ecological Edge Effect Framework for Boundary Interventions in Blended Science Learning
proposals.motivation: Blended learning often aims for seamless integration, yet students and teachers report cognitive and epistemic barriers when transitioning between digital and physical spaces, leading to disorientation and increased extraneous load. Pre-2026 research, including Tsai's work on cognitive load in AR and barriers to technology integration, highlights these challenges but lacks design frameworks that leverage the boundary itself as a pedagogical resource. This study addresses the need for principled design to manage, rather than erase, the inherent friction between learning ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: Intentionally designed boundary interventions, inspired by the management of ecological edge effects, will reduce disruptive cognitive load and enhance students' metacognitive awareness and self-efficacy by clarifying the distinct epistemic affordances of digital and physical learning spaces.
proposals.method: The study will employ a design-based research (DBR) methodology across multiple iterations. We will first conduct a belief and epistemic network analysis of student and teacher interview data to map perceived barriers at the digital-physical boundary. Then, we will co-design specific 'boundary interventions' (e.g., context-switching rituals, dual-realm reflective objects) with instructors. Their efficacy will be tested using a mixed-methods approach: measuring cognitive load subtypes (via validated scales) and science learning self-efficacy, combined with sequential pattern mining of digital and physical activity logs to detect behavioral transitions and engagement in the boundary zone.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel theoretical framework for blended learning design derived from landscape ecology, moving beyond integration rhetoric. Practically, it will yield a toolkit of tested boundary intervention prototypes. It will also advance methodological integration by linking epistemic belief analysis with temporal behavior pattern mining to understand boundary navigation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fundamentally reconceptualizing the online-physical boundary not as a problem to be solved but as a designed 'ecotone' with unique pedagogical value, applying a cross-disciplinary analogy not previously used in learning sciences. It shifts the design goal from seamlessness to productive, metacognition-inducing friction.

proposals.title: Designing for Productive Friction: An Epistemic Design Framework for Seamless Integration of Online and Real-World Science Learning
proposals.motivation: The rapid shift to online and blended learning, accelerated by events like the COVID-19 pandemic, has revealed a critical challenge: the seamless integration of digital and physical learning activities can lead to cognitive overload and superficial engagement, as noted in prior work on barriers to technology-integrated instruction. There is a pressing need to move beyond simplistic notions of seamlessness and instead design intentional, pedagogically grounded points of connection and disconnection between modalities to foster deeper knowledge construction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a learning design framework which strategically introduces 'productive friction'—planned epistemic barriers and clear contextual boundaries between online and real-world tasks—will lead to improved metacognitive awareness, more coherent knowledge integration, and higher science learning self-efficacy compared to designs aiming for maximal seamlessness.
proposals.method: This design-based research study will employ a mixed-methods approach. We will develop and iteratively test a novel epistemic design framework with undergraduate science students. The framework will guide the creation of learning sequences combining AR-assisted simulations (using Unity-based applications) with physical lab work. Data collection will include multimodal logs (eye-tracking and interaction data from the AR platform), pre/post measures of self-efficacy and cognitive load (using validated scales like the NASA-TLX), and in-depth interviews to capture student and teacher beliefs about integration. Analysis will use latent profile analysis to identify student profiles based on cognitive load and epistemic engagement patterns, complemented by qualitative thematic analysis of interview data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a theoretically grounded, practical design framework for blended learning that re-conceptualizes 'seamlessness' as a pedagogical choice rather than a technical goal. It will provide empirical evidence on how specific design features (e.g., structured reflection prompts at modality transitions, contrasting virtual and physical data) impact cognitive and epistemic outcomes, offering actionable guidance for teachers facing integration barriers.
proposals.novelty: While existing research often focuses on removing barriers to integration, this proposal innovates by deliberately designing for specific, productive forms of friction as catalysts for learning. It uniquely combines Tsai's expertise in epistemic beliefs, cognitive load, and barriers to technology integration to address a core, unresolved tension in post-pandemic educational design.

proposals.title: Designing for Agency and Adaptation: A Study of Asynchronous State-Signaling in Science Learning Environments
proposals.motivation: Fully adaptive learning environments often rely on real-time sensing or continuous data streams, which raise significant privacy concerns and are not always technically feasible, especially in asynchronous or resource-constrained settings. Conversely, static online learning environments, as highlighted in prior work on massive distance education, present barriers to engagement by failing to respond to individual learner states. This creates a critical need for adaptive designs that balance responsiveness with learner control and practical implementation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a learning environment using structured, periodic student self-reports (signaling) to trigger pre-computed instructional adaptations will lead to higher learning self-efficacy and more productive engagement patterns than a static environment, while achieving comparable learning outcomes to a sensor-driven system but with greater perceived learner agency.
proposals.method: We will design a web-based science learning module (e.g., on molecular bonding) with three conditions: static, sensor-driven (using webcam-based facial expression analysis via pre-2026 Affectiva or OpenFace toolkits for engagement estimation), and the novel signaling-based condition. The signaling system will employ periodic, embedded micro-surveys using the SRL (Self-Regulated Learning) microanalytic protocol to capture understanding, confidence, and cognitive load. These signals will be processed by a rule-based decision engine (implemented with the Cognitive Tutor Authoring Tools framework) to select from a pre-authored library of adaptive responses, such as problem difficulty adjustment or provision of worked examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a concrete design framework for 'asynchronous adaptation,' providing a viable path for responsive learning technologies that uphold privacy and learner agency. It will yield empirical evidence comparing the efficacy of signaling-based adaptation against dominant paradigms, directly informing the design of future distance and blended learning systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly decoupling adaptation from real-time monitoring by centering deliberate learner signaling as the control mechanism, a hybrid approach that moves beyond the dichotomy of static versus sensor-driven systems. It integrates epistemic and self-regulative constructs (confidence, load) directly into the adaptation logic, bridging research on student beliefs and instructional design.

proposals.title: A Participatory Meta-Framework for Contextually Adaptive Smart Learning Environments: Co-Design, Validation, and Impact Across Diverse Educational Settings
proposals.motivation: Smart Learning Environments (SLEs) often fail to scale or transfer effectively because they are designed with a one-size-fits-all approach, ignoring critical local contextual factors like teacher beliefs, cultural norms, and resource constraints. This is evident in the documented barriers to technology-integrated and massive online instruction, where generic solutions exacerbate rather than solve problems of engagement and efficacy. A methodology is urgently needed to systematically generate SLE designs that are both evidence-based and deeply contextualized.
proposals.hypothesis: A participatory meta-framework, which provides a structured co-design process and core principles rather than a fixed design, will enable the creation of SLEs that demonstrate higher levels of local stakeholder ownership, pedagogical fit, and measurable learning impact across diverse educational contexts compared to top-down, standardized SLE implementations.
proposals.method: The research will employ a design-based research (DBR) methodology across a portfolio of contrasting contexts (e.g., high-resource vs. low-resource, Eastern vs. Western pedagogical traditions). The core technical approach involves developing and iteratively refining a co-design toolkit featuring (1) context-mapping instruments based on Tsai's prior work on epistemic beliefs and barrier identification, (2) principle-based design cards derived from pre-2026 meta-analyses on SLE effectiveness, and (3) a lightweight adaptive logic architecture (e.g., using rule-based systems or simple Bayesian networks pre-2026) to prototype context-aware activity sequencing. Impact assessment will triangulate learning analytics (e.g., behavior patterns, cognitive load), self-efficacy surveys, and stakeholder interviews.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, practical methodology for the contextual adaptation of SLEs, moving the field beyond prescriptive frameworks. It will generate a rich, comparative dataset on how core SLE principles manifest differently across contexts, offering new insights into the interaction between technology, pedagogy, and culture. The output will be a robust co-design toolkit for researchers and practitioners.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the unit of output from a static SLE design to a generative *process* for creating such designs, explicitly built to handle contextual adversariality (e.g., conflicting stakeholder beliefs or infrastructural barriers). It integrates the study of epistemic and contextual barriers directly into the design mechanism of the SLE itself, which is a non-obvious synthesis of Tsai's prior work on barriers and learning environments.

proposals.title: From Pedagogical Gaps to Technological Fits: A Needs-First Decision Matrix for Smart Learning Environment Design
proposals.motivation: Despite rapid investment in educational technologies like AR and AI, there is a persistent gap between technological capabilities and genuine pedagogical needs, often leading to ineffective or underused tools. Prior research, including our own on barriers to technology-integrated instruction, shows that adoption failures frequently stem from a technology-first approach rather than a needs-first analysis. This project addresses the critical need for a systematic, evidence-based methodology to align emerging technologies with validated, high-impact pedagogical failures in real-world learning contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured framework that first identifies and prioritizes concrete pedagogical gaps through mixed-methods analysis, and then evaluates technologies against criteria of cognitive fit and scalability, will yield a more effective and pragmatically adopted mapping of tools to learning needs than current ad-hoc or trend-driven approaches.
proposals.method: We will first conduct ethnographic studies and instructor interviews in target STEM classrooms to identify pedagogical failures, which will be triangulated with learning analytics data (e.g., sequence mining of interaction logs) to quantify their impact. Using this data, we will employ a Delphi method with learning scientists and practitioners to prioritize needs. For the evaluation framework, we will adapt multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) algorithms, weighting factors like cognitive load alignment (informed by CLT), scalability, and accessibility, to create a decision matrix that scores candidate technologies (e.g., AR for spatial visualization, AI tutors for formative feedback) against each validated need.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, evidence-based decision matrix and prototyping methodology for educators and developers, moving beyond generic best practices. It will contribute a novel theoretical lens that frames technology integration as a problem of matching specific cognitive and pedagogical requirements, thereby advancing the epistemic understanding of 'fit' in learning design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies barriers or evaluates single technologies, this research innovates by developing a systematic, bidirectional mapping methodology that starts from empirically-prioritized pedagogical gaps and uses a structured cognitive-fit evaluation to recommend technologies, reversing the common technology-push paradigm.

proposals.title: Mapping the Diffusion Pathway: A Predictive Model of AI-Augmented Reality Adoption Barriers in K-12 and Higher Education
proposals.motivation: The convergence of AI and AR promises transformative smart learning ecosystems, yet their real-world adoption faces significant, unquantified barriers. Pre-2026 research on technology integration often treats these barriers generically or examines single technologies in isolation, lacking a predictive framework for the complex, multi-stage adoption of integrated AI-AR systems. Understanding the specific epistemic and practical hurdles across different adopter categories (e.g., teachers, administrators) is critical to avoid failed implementations and wasted investment.
proposals.hypothesis: The diffusion of integrated AI-AR systems in education will follow a non-linear, sector-dependent pathway, where adoption velocity is primarily constrained by a core set of interlocking epistemic barriers (e.g., beliefs about knowledge construction) and infrastructural challenges, which differ significantly between early adopters and the early majority.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods study will first employ a systematic literature review (2015-2025) to catalog known barriers to AI and AR in education. Second, a cross-sectional survey, grounded in Diffusion of Innovations theory and the Technology Acceptance Model, will quantify perceived barriers, self-efficacy, and adoption intent among in-service teachers (n=300) and university instructors (n=200). Third, predictive agent-based modeling (using NetLogo) will simulate diffusion pathways under different intervention scenarios, with model parameters calibrated by survey data and expert interviews.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a validated, predictive model of AI-AR adoption, identifying critical intervention points for policymakers and instructional designers. It will extend diffusion theory by explicitly modeling the interaction between teachers' epistemic beliefs and technological infrastructure as a joint determinant of adoption, moving beyond simple attitudinal predictors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by the researcher and others identifies barriers, this study uniquely synthesizes these findings into a dynamic, simulatable diffusion model specifically for the nascent AI-AR fusion, offering a forward-looking, evidence-based planning tool rather than a retrospective analysis.

proposals.title: Validating Smart Learning Design Criteria: An Empirical Study of Epistemic Engagement and Cognitive Load in AR-Enhanced Science Classrooms
proposals.motivation: The concept of 'smart learning' is widely discussed in educational technology literature, but its proposed features—such as context-aware, adaptive, and personalized support—are often abstract and lack rigorous empirical validation in real classroom settings. Given the rapid integration of technologies like Augmented Reality (AR) in science education, it is critical to test which specific smart learning criteria genuinely enhance learning processes and outcomes, moving beyond theoretical frameworks to evidence-based design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that science learning environments designed with specific, operationalized smart learning criteria (e.g., adaptive scaffolding, real-time feedback) will lead to measurably higher levels of productive epistemic engagement and lower extraneous cognitive load compared to standard technology-integrated instruction, and that these effects will be mediated by students' self-efficacy.
proposals.method: The study will employ a mixed-methods, quasi-experimental design in undergraduate science courses. A treatment group will use an AR learning module explicitly built with features mapped to established smart learning criteria (e.g., using a rule-based algorithm for adaptive hint delivery based on real-time performance). A control group will use a standard AR module without these adaptive features. Data collection will combine multimodal sources: pre/post tests of conceptual knowledge, validated surveys on cognitive load and self-efficacy, log-file analysis of interaction patterns, and stimulated recall interviews to capture epistemic beliefs and perceived barriers. Analysis will use multivariate statistics and epistemic network analysis to link design features to process and outcome variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, evidence-based validation of key smart learning design criteria, directly informing the development of more effective educational technologies. It will contribute to learning sciences by elucidating the mechanisms—through cognitive load and epistemic engagement—by which smart features influence science learning, offering a refined model for future design-based research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by the researcher and others has identified barriers and measured cognitive load in technology use, this study uniquely operationalizes abstract smart learning criteria into testable instructional features and directly investigates their impact on the under-explored construct of epistemic engagement in authentic classroom contexts.

proposals.title: Diagnosing and Mitigating Epistemic Barriers in Smart Learning Environments: A Multi-Modal, Process-Oriented Approach
proposals.motivation: Smart learning environments (SLEs) promise personalized, adaptive instruction, but pre-2026 research, including Tsai's own work, identifies persistent barriers to effective technology integration, often stemming from teacher and student beliefs and epistemic challenges. A critical, unresolved issue is the lack of fine-grained, real-time diagnostics for how SLEs might inadvertently create or exacerbate epistemic barriers (e.g., fostering superficial interaction, misunderstanding AI-generated feedback), which undermine meaningful knowledge construction in science learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, identifiable patterns of multi-modal interaction data (log-file, eye-tracking, and physiological signals) within a science-focused SLE correlate with the emergence of epistemic barriers, and that a design intervention providing real-time, metacognitive scaffolding based on these patterns will reduce such barriers and improve learning outcomes.
proposals.method: We will design a quasi-experimental study with in-service science teachers and their undergraduate students. Using a pre-existing AR/VR science learning platform, we will instrument it to capture multi-modal process data. We will employ sequence mining algorithms (e.g., Lag Sequential Analysis) on interaction logs and computer vision techniques for eye-gaze analysis to identify behavioral-epistemic profiles. A real-time dashboard will be developed using a rule-based architecture (IF-THEN rules derived from the mined profiles) to trigger contextual prompts (e.g., "Pause and explain your reasoning") when barrier-indicative patterns are detected. Learning outcomes, self-efficacy, and epistemic engagement will be measured via pre/post-tests and validated self-report instruments.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, process-oriented framework for diagnosing epistemic barriers in SLEs, moving beyond post-hoc surveys. It will yield a proof-of-concept, theory-driven design principle for metacognitive scaffolding that is responsive to real-time learner states, directly addressing the 'how' of mitigating barriers identified in prior barrier-comparison and online learning challenge studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of multi-modal analytics with epistemic cognition theory to dynamically diagnose learning barriers *as they occur*, and in the design of an intervention that targets the epistemic dimension of cognitive load and self-efficacy within SLEs, an approach not yet synthesized in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Multi-Factor Adaptive Learning System: Integrating Epistemic Beliefs, Cognitive Load, and Contextual Barriers for Personalized Science Education
proposals.motivation: Current context-aware learning systems primarily adapt based on location or simple performance metrics, neglecting critical learner-internal and external factors that significantly influence outcomes. Pre-2026 research by Tsai and others highlights the importance of students' epistemic beliefs, cognitive load profiles, and perceived contextual barriers (e.g., technological, instructional) in science learning, yet these are rarely integrated into a unified adaptive design. This gap limits the personalization and effectiveness of technology-enhanced learning, especially in complex environments like augmented reality (AR) or massive online courses.
proposals.hypothesis: A learning system that dynamically adapts content and scaffolding based on a integrated model of a learner's real-time cognitive load, epistemic beliefs, and perceived contextual barriers will lead to higher learning self-efficacy and more productive learning behaviors than systems adapting on basic performance or context alone.
proposals.method: We will design and develop a prototype adaptive learning system for a core science concept. The system's backend will use a rule-based and weighted multi-attribute decision-making algorithm to process a real-time factor profile. This profile integrates: (1) cognitive load inferred from embedded performance and interaction data (e.g., time-on-task, error rates) within an AR simulation, (2) pre-assessed epistemic beliefs via a validated questionnaire, and (3) dynamically reported contextual barriers via brief in-system prompts (e.g., distraction, technical difficulty). The algorithm will trigger specific adaptations, such as adjusting task complexity, providing epistemic refutation texts, or offering alternative task formats.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel design framework and proof-of-concept architecture for multi-factor adaptive learning systems, moving beyond simplistic context-awareness. It will provide empirical evidence on how the interplay of cognitive, epistemic, and contextual factors can be leveraged for personalization, offering practical insights for designers and teachers aiming to overcome known barriers in technology-integrated instruction.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines factors like cognitive load or epistemic beliefs in isolation, this proposal's novelty lies in their real-time integration within an operational system's adaptation logic. It uniquely connects the literature on internal cognitive/affective states with external contextual barriers into a single, actionable learner model for dynamic intervention.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Epistemically Aligned Smart Learning Environments: A Participatory Study with Science Teachers and Students
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on technology-integrated instruction, including Dr. Tsai's own work, consistently identifies teacher and student beliefs, epistemic practices, and perceived barriers as critical to adoption and effectiveness. Smart learning environments (SLEs) often fail because they are designed without deep engagement with end-users' pedagogical and epistemic needs, leading to tools that are misaligned with authentic classroom practices.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured participatory design (PD) process involving teachers and students as co-designers will yield SLE prototypes that are more epistemically aligned with science learning practices and perceived as having fewer implementation barriers compared to expert-driven designs.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase participatory design study with 6-8 secondary science teachers and 15-20 of their students. Following established HCI PD methods (e.g., future workshops, layered elaboration, prototyping with tools like Figma), we will co-create wireframes and functional mock-ups for an augmented reality (AR)-assisted science inquiry module. Data will include audio/video recordings of design sessions, design artifacts, and pre/post surveys measuring participants' epistemic beliefs about technology, perceived barriers (using scales adapted from Tsai's prior work), and cognitive load expectations. Qualitative analysis will use thematic coding of design rationale, while quantitative analysis will compare barrier perceptions pre- and post-design.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a detailed case study and methodological framework for applying HCI participatory design in educational technology development, specifically for SLEs. It will generate empirically grounded design principles that link co-design processes to outcomes in epistemic alignment and reduced perceived barriers, offering actionable guidance for learning environment designers.
proposals.novelty: While PD is known in HCI, its systematic application to address the epistemic and belief-based barriers documented in educational technology literature, particularly for co-creating SLEs with both teachers and students, is novel. It moves beyond identifying barriers to actively involving users in designing solutions that mitigate them.

proposals.title: Contradictions and Expansive Learning in Smart Science Classrooms: An Activity Theory Analysis of Technology-Integrated Instructional Systems
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of smart technologies (AR, mobile devices, LMS) into science education creates complex learning activity systems. Pre-2026 research by Tsai and others identifies specific barriers and cognitive load profiles, but often analyzes components in isolation. A systemic framework is needed to understand the dynamic interactions and inherent contradictions between tools, rules, community, and division of labor that ultimately shape student knowledge construction and teacher practice.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Activity Theory as an analytical lens will reveal primary and secondary contradictions within smart learning activity systems that explain observed barriers to technology integration (e.g., teacher beliefs, student self-efficacy) and predict opportunities for expansive learning and system redesign.
proposals.method: This qualitative design-based research will employ Engeström's third-generation Activity Theory model. We will conduct multi-voiced analysis of activity systems in secondary school science classrooms using smart AR applications. Data from teacher interviews (focusing on epistemic beliefs and perceived barriers), student surveys (self-efficacy, cognitive load), and video-recorded behavior patterns will be mapped onto the activity system nodes (subject, object, tools, rules, community, division of labor, outcome). Contradictions will be identified through thematic analysis of tensions within and between interacting activity systems (e.g., teacher's instructional system vs. student's learning system).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, systemic explanatory model for barriers and facilitators in technology-enhanced science learning, moving beyond listing factors to modeling their interdependencies. It will offer educators and designers a structured framework (the activity system model) for diagnosing and resolving systemic contradictions to improve instructional design and foster expansive learning cycles.
proposals.novelty: While Activity Theory has been used in HCI and organizational studies, its application to analyze the specific contradictions within *smart* science learning environments, synthesizing Tsai's prior work on beliefs, barriers, and cognitive load into a unified systemic model, represents a novel theoretical advance in educational technology research.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Context: A Participatory Framework for Inferring Learning Situations from Multimodal Data to Support Science Education
proposals.motivation: Current learning analytics often rely on coarse digital footprints (e.g., LMS clicks) that fail to capture the rich, situated context of learning, especially in mobile, blended, or distance environments. This gap limits meaningful pedagogical intervention. Furthermore, prior work by the researcher and others identifies significant teacher and student concerns about data privacy, interpretability, and relevance as barriers to adopting analytics technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: A context-aware analytics framework, co-designed with educators and students to infer learning situations (e.g., focused study, collaborative troubleshooting) from fused multimodal data and learner-initiated ground truth, will produce more pedagogically actionable insights and be perceived as more trustworthy and useful than existing diagnostic models.
proposals.method: We will develop a mobile/web application to collect passive multimodal data (device use logs, GPS-derived location, anonymized ambient audio features) alongside periodic, learner-controlled Experience Sampling Method (ESM) prompts. A multi-modal fusion model, likely using a late-fusion neural network architecture, will be trained to classify learning contexts. Crucially, the feature set, model outputs (context labels), and a dashboard of interventions will be iteratively defined and refined through participatory design workshops with science teachers and undergraduate students, informed by the researcher's prior work on epistemic beliefs and technology barriers.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel, ethically-grounded methodological framework for context inference in learning analytics that prioritizes participatory design. It will produce a validated, context-aware system prototype and new knowledge about how co-design shapes the perceived utility and ethical acceptability of analytics in science education.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond clinical 'diagnosis' of cognitive states by focusing on inferring externally observable, pedagogically relevant situations co-defined with stakeholders, and it integrates an ethical governance layer (transparency, user control) as a core, co-designed component of the technical system from its inception.

proposals.title: Modeling Epistemic Engagement and Cognitive Load in Science Learning via Passive Mobile Sensing: A Digital Phenotyping Approach
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, understanding student learning states (e.g., cognitive load, epistemic engagement) in digital environments relies heavily on self-report or intrusive measures, which are reactive and lack temporal granularity. The shift to massive and mobile learning, as noted in Tsai's work, creates a need for scalable, unobtrusive methods to model these latent constructs and identify real-time barriers to learning. Digital phenotyping, proven in mobile health for modeling behavioral and affective states, offers a promising but unexplored methodology for educational science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that passive sensor data (e.g., phone use patterns, ambient audio, location) and device interaction logs from students' mobile devices can be used to construct digital phenotypes that reliably model fluctuations in self-reported cognitive load and epistemic engagement during science learning tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Beiwe research platform for education, collecting continuous passive data (GPS, accelerometer, screen events, app usage) from undergraduate students during an augmented reality-assisted science module. Self-reported cognitive load and epistemic beliefs surveys will provide ground-truth labels. We will employ feature extraction methods common in mHealth (e.g., circadian rhythms, mobility variance, keystroke dynamics) and use supervised machine learning algorithms, specifically Random Forests and LSTMs, to classify high/low cognitive load states and epistemic behavior patterns. Model performance will be validated against behavioral logs and learning outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodological framework for applying digital phenotyping to educational research, enabling the fine-grained, longitudinal analysis of learning states. It will provide empirical evidence on the connection between passive behavioral signatures and key educational constructs, offering teachers and instructional designers a tool for early identification of student struggle and disengagement.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of a clinical behavioral sensing paradigm (digital phenotyping) to model epistemic and cognitive constructs in science education, moving beyond simple activity tracking to infer complex learning states from passive, multi-modal data streams.

proposals.title: Inferring Learner Context from Interaction Logs: A Multimodal Deep Learning Approach to Personalize Science Instruction
proposals.motivation: Effective learning is highly contextual, yet digital learning systems often operate with minimal awareness of a student's physical or social environment, a significant barrier to adaptive instruction. This gap is critical in science education, where hands-on, collaborative, or mobile learning (e.g., AR-assisted labs) is common. Inferring context from learner actions can enable systems to dynamically adjust support, addressing challenges in distance education and technology-integrated instruction identified in the researcher's prior work.
proposals.hypothesis: A multimodal deep learning model, trained on fine-grained interaction logs and basic sensor data, can accurately infer key contextual dimensions (e.g., physical setting, social configuration, available tools) of a learner during science learning activities. The inferred context will significantly predict variations in cognitive load and self-efficacy beyond interaction patterns alone.
proposals.method: We will collect multimodal data (interface clickstreams, timing, AR object manipulation sequences, and anonymized audio amplitude for social cue detection) from undergraduate students using an AR science simulation. A hybrid neural architecture will be developed, combining a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to model temporal action sequences with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) branch to process aggregated interaction features. This model will be trained to classify contexts (pre-defined labels like 'solitary', 'collaborative group', 'distracted environment') verified by periodic ecological momentary assessments.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, empirically validated computational framework for implicit context sensing in learning analytics, moving beyond post-hoc analysis to real-time inference. It will provide new insights into how contextual barriers manifest in digital learning behaviors, directly informing the design of more adaptive and context-aware educational technology.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes interactions for learning outcomes, this proposal inverts the problem to infer the external context *causing* those interactions. It uniquely integrates the researcher's expertise in epistemic barriers, cognitive load, and AR learning with multimodal deep learning for context inference, a nascent approach in educational technology pre-2026.

proposals.title: Cross-Cultural Validation of the Information Commitment Survey: A Multi-National Study of Epistemic Engagement in Science Learning
proposals.motivation: The Information Commitment Survey (ICS) is a promising tool for assessing students' epistemic engagement—their willingness to evaluate and integrate information—in technology-enhanced science learning, as developed in Taiwan. However, its structural validity and measurement invariance across diverse cultural and educational contexts remain untested. Given the global push for digital and online science education, understanding how epistemic beliefs and commitments manifest differently is crucial for designing equitable and effective learning technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: The ICS model, measuring the latent constructs of information evaluation and integration, will demonstrate configural and metric invariance across student populations in Taiwan, Germany, and the United States, but scalar invariance will not be fully achieved, indicating culturally influenced interpretations of specific survey items.
proposals.method: The study will employ a cross-sectional survey design, administering the validated ICS instrument to undergraduate science students in Taiwan (n≈400), Germany (n≈400), and the USA (n≈400). Data analysis will use Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with Mplus software. We will first conduct Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) to test the original model in each sample, followed by multi-group CFA to rigorously test for measurement invariance across the three groups using a sequential constraint approach (configural, metric, scalar). Post-hoc analyses will examine item-level differential item functioning (DIF) using the Wald test to identify specific items contributing to non-invariance.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide robust empirical evidence on the cross-cultural applicability of a key epistemic engagement instrument. It will advance the field of learning sciences by clarifying how the construct of information commitment is universally understood versus culturally situated, offering critical insights for researchers and instructional designers aiming to adapt technology-enhanced learning interventions for global audiences.
proposals.novelty: While cross-cultural studies of general epistemic beliefs exist, this is the first to rigorously test the invariance of a model specifically targeting *information commitment* within science learning contexts, directly linking cultural validation to practical educational technology design challenges identified in the researcher's prior work on barriers to technology integration.

proposals.title: Tracking the Development of Epistemic Vigilance: A Longitudinal Study of How Undergraduate Science Students' Evaluative Standards for Online Scientific Information Evolve with Digital Experience
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of scientific information online presents a critical challenge for learners, who must navigate varying levels of credibility and authority. Pre-2026 research, including Tsai's work on epistemic barriers and technology-integrated instruction, highlights that students often lack sophisticated evaluative standards for web materials, impacting their learning and knowledge construction. Understanding how these standards develop over time is essential for designing effective digital literacy interventions in science education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that students' evaluative standards for web-based scientific materials will progress through identifiable, non-linear stages, moving from surface-feature reliance (e.g., website aesthetics) to more sophisticated epistemic criteria (e.g., source authority, evidence quality, corroboration), and that this development is mediated by their level of internet experience, science learning self-efficacy, and instructional exposure.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-semester longitudinal mixed-methods study with ~150 undergraduate science majors. Data collection will involve: 1) repeated think-aloud protocols where participants evaluate curated and authentic web materials, analyzed via epistemic network analysis (ENA) to model the structure of their evaluative reasoning; 2) pre- and post-task surveys measuring self-efficacy, epistemic beliefs, and cognitive load; and 3) digital trace data from a browser extension logging participants' voluntary online science information-seeking behavior patterns. Growth curve modeling will be used to analyze developmental trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, process-oriented model of how evaluative standards for digital scientific information develop, moving beyond static snapshots. It will directly inform the design of technology-integrated science curricula that target specific epistemic barriers and foster critical online inquiry skills, contributing to the fields of science education and digital literacy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies barriers to technology use, this study uniquely traces the dynamic *development* of evaluative standards themselves, combining fine-grained epistemic analysis with longitudinal behavioral data to reveal the interplay between experience, belief, and skill.

proposals.title: Disciplinary Epistemic Cultures: A Comparative Study of Information Commitments Among Undergraduate Science, Humanities, and Professional Students
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on epistemic beliefs and information literacy often treats students as a homogeneous group, yet learning is deeply contextualized within academic disciplines. Understanding how students' information commitments—their criteria for evaluating, trusting, and using information—vary by disciplinary culture is crucial for designing effective instructional technology and pedagogical interventions. This is particularly relevant as digital learning environments and online resources become ubiquitous, raising new challenges for fostering critical evaluation skills across different fields of study.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that undergraduate students' information commitments differ systematically across the epistemic cultures of the sciences, humanities, and professional fields (e.g., business, engineering). Specifically, we predict science students will prioritize empirical evidence and replicability, humanities students will prioritize interpretive authority and contextual coherence, and professional students will prioritize pragmatic utility and expert consensus.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods design. First, a validated Epistemic Beliefs and Information Commitments questionnaire will be administered to a stratified sample of 600+ undergraduates from target disciplines. Second, we will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with a subset of 30-45 students, presenting them with discipline-relevant and cross-disciplinary information scenarios (e.g., a scientific preprint, a historical analysis, a market report). Interview transcripts will be analyzed using thematic analysis and epistemic network analysis (ENA) to model the structure of their reasoning. Quantitative data will be analyzed using MANOVA and cluster analysis to identify distinct commitment profiles.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, empirically grounded map of how disciplinary training shapes fundamental epistemic stances toward information. It will contribute to the learning sciences by linking macro-level disciplinary norms to micro-level student cognition. The findings will directly inform the design of more effective, discipline-sensitive instructional supports, library resources, and digital learning tools that align with students' existing epistemic frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has compared general epistemic beliefs, this study explicitly investigates the under-explored construct of 'information commitments' as a situated, practice-oriented form of epistemic cognition. The direct, multi-method comparison across three broad epistemic cultures (sciences, humanities, professions) using scenario-based interviews and ENA moves beyond broad surveys to capture the contextual reasoning processes, offering a novel integration of Tsai's expertise in epistemic barriers with disciplinary learning research.

proposals.title: Modeling Epistemic Search: A Cognitive Task Analysis of Expert and Novice Web Search Strategies in Science Learning Contexts
proposals.motivation: The internet is a primary source for science learning, yet students often struggle with effective information search and evaluation, a critical epistemic practice. Pre-2026 research on technology-integrated learning and cognitive load highlights barriers to effective online learning but lacks fine-grained models of the cognitive processes underlying successful search. Understanding the gap between expert and novice search strategies is essential for designing better instructional scaffolds.
proposals.hypothesis: Expert searchers (e.g., experienced researchers/science teachers) will employ distinct, more efficient cognitive task structures characterized by superior query formulation, systematic result evaluation, and adaptive strategy shifts compared to novice students, and these differences will be linked to their epistemic beliefs about online knowledge.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods Cognitive Task Analysis (CTA) approach. First, we will conduct in-depth, think-aloud protocol interviews with experts (n=15) and novices (n=30) as they complete authentic science inquiry search tasks. Screen/eye-tracking and log data will be captured. Verbal reports and behavioral patterns will be coded using a structured scheme derived from pre-2026 information foraging and epistemic cognition frameworks. The analysis will focus on decomposing tasks into goal hierarchies, decision points, and critical cues. We will then use process mining algorithms (e.g., HeuristicsMiner in ProM) to model and statistically compare the workflow patterns and cognitive pathways of each group.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a detailed cognitive model of expert web search for science learning, identifying teachable strategic components. It will contribute to the learning sciences by linking epistemic beliefs and search behavior, offering a foundation for targeted instructional design to reduce cognitive load and improve students' self-efficacy in online research environments.
proposals.novelty: While CTA is established in expertise studies, its application to model the cognitive processes of web search within science education is novel. This approach moves beyond comparing surface behaviors or self-reports to uncover the underlying goal structures and decision-making heuristics that differentiate expertise, directly integrating Tsai's themes of epistemic barriers and technology design.

proposals.title: Integrating Cognitive Load Theory and Epistemic Network Analysis: A Model-Driven Framework for Interpreting Analytics in Augmented Reality Science Learning
proposals.motivation: Educational analytics often rely on correlational data mining without a strong theoretical basis for interpreting student behaviors, especially in complex technology-enhanced environments like Augmented Reality (AR). This limits teachers' ability to understand the underlying cognitive and epistemic processes behind learning patterns, a gap highlighted in pre-2026 research on barriers to technology integration. A theory-driven model is needed to translate analytics into actionable insights about learning and self-efficacy.
