proposals.hypothesis: A computational model integrating Cognitive Load Theory (CLT) dimensions (intrinsic, extraneous, germane) with epistemic network analysis of student discourse and action logs will provide a more valid and interpretable framework for classifying student learning profiles and predicting science learning self-efficacy than purely data-driven clustering approaches.
proposals.method: The study will design an AR-based science learning module. Data streams (log files, screen recordings, pre/post self-efficacy scales) will be collected. A model-driven algorithm will be developed where features are explicitly defined by CLT (e.g., element interactivity for intrinsic load, interface manipulations for extraneous load) and epistemic frame markers. These features will guide a hybrid analytical pipeline using finite-state machines to segment learning phases, epistemic network analysis to model knowledge construction, and theory-informed clustering (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Models with priors) to identify load profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable analytical framework that explicitly links educational theory (CLT, epistemic cognition) to algorithm design. It will provide educators with a diagnostic tool to move beyond behavioral patterns to infer cognitive states, offering specific guidance for instructional adjustments in AR environments to manage load and support self-efficacy.
proposals.novelty: While analytics and CLT have been used separately, this research novelly operationalizes CLT dimensions and epistemic framing as direct, computable constraints for feature engineering and model interpretation in educational data mining, moving beyond post-hoc theoretical explanations.

proposals.title: Bridging Theory and Practice: A Cognitive Load and Epistemic Engagement Framework for Evaluating Augmented Reality in High School Science Inquiry
proposals.motivation: The rapid adoption of immersive technologies like Augmented Reality (AR) in science education, accelerated by shifts to online learning, often outpaces rigorous pedagogical evaluation. Pre-2026 research, including Tsai's own work, identifies barriers to effective technology integration and highlights the need to understand how such tools affect core learning processes like cognitive load and epistemic engagement. Without empirical testing against established learning theories, there is a risk that new technologies become novel distractions rather than effective learning tools.
proposals.hypothesis: AR science learning activities designed with explicit scaffolds to manage intrinsic and germane cognitive load, based on Cognitive Load Theory and the ICAP framework (Interactive, Constructive, Active, Passive), will lead to measurably higher levels of productive epistemic engagement and conceptual understanding compared to AR activities lacking such theory-driven design.
proposals.method: A mixed-methods design-based research study will be conducted in high school biology classrooms. Two versions of an AR module on cellular processes will be developed: one with theory-based scaffolds (e.g., segmented information, worked examples, prompts for self-explanation) and a control version without. Data collection will integrate multimodal sensors (eye-tracking and log data from AR devices) to infer real-time cognitive load, alongside pre/post-tests of conceptual knowledge, the Epistemic Cognition Questionnaire, and stimulated recall interviews. Analysis will use multivariate analysis of covariance (MANCOVA) for quantitative measures and thematic analysis for qualitative data, correlating behavioral patterns from sensor data with learning outcomes and epistemic beliefs.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, multi-dimensional framework for evaluating educational technologies that links specific design features to theoretical constructs of learning. It will offer concrete design principles for AR that are grounded in cognitive and epistemic theory, moving beyond usability to focus on pedagogical efficacy. The findings will directly address known barriers to technology integration by giving teachers and designers a theory-based rationale for implementation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines cognitive load or epistemic beliefs in isolation, this study uniquely integrates them within a real-time, multimodal assessment protocol in an authentic classroom setting. It moves from identifying barriers to testing a specific, theory-driven design solution that targets those barriers, using sensor data to objectively capture the interplay between cognitive processing and epistemic engagement during AR-assisted inquiry.

proposals.title: Designing and Evaluating a Home-Based Augmented Reality Science Learning Intervention: Shifting the Locus of Epistemic Change
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Tsai's own work, identifies significant barriers to technology integration in formal school settings and highlights the challenges of massive online learning. However, the home environment, as a primary site for informal and self-directed learning, remains an underexplored locus for structured educational intervention. This is crucial for designing equitable and scalable learning systems that extend beyond the classroom, especially in contexts where school-based technology access is limited.
proposals.hypothesis: A carefully designed, home-based Augmented Reality (AR) science learning intervention, which scaffolds parental epistemic support and student self-regulation, will lead to greater gains in students' science learning self-efficacy and more sophisticated epistemic beliefs about science than a comparable school-based implementation.
proposals.method: The study will employ a mixed-methods, quasi-experimental design. A novel AR learning module on a core science concept (e.g., ecosystems) will be developed using a platform like Unity3D with Vuforia. The intervention group will use the module at home with guided support materials for parents, while the control group uses it in school. Data collection will integrate pre/post surveys on self-efficacy and epistemic beliefs, log-file analysis of in-app behavior patterns (using sequence mining algorithms), and post-intervention interviews with a subset of parent-child dyads to understand epistemic interactions and barriers.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated design framework for home-centered digital learning interventions that explicitly address epistemic and motivational factors. It will generate new insights into the relationship between learning context (home vs. school), technology-mediated behavior patterns, and the development of science self-efficacy, extending Tsai's previous research on cognitive load and barriers in AR and online environments.
proposals.novelty: While much educational design research focuses on the classroom, this proposal deliberately inverts the paradigm by designing for the home as the primary intervention site, systematically comparing its efficacy and epistemic dynamics against the school context. It uniquely integrates the analysis of student learning behavior with the scaffolding of parental epistemic support within a home-based AR environment.

proposals.title: Student-Generated Context-Aware Questions: An Epistemic and Technological Intervention to Invert Assessment in Science Learning
proposals.motivation: Traditional teacher-controlled assessment can create epistemic barriers, positioning students as passive recipients of knowledge rather than active constructors. This is particularly salient in science education, where deep conceptual understanding requires engagement with the material's context. A student-generated question model, facilitated by technology, could transform assessment into a collaborative, reflective learning activity, addressing known challenges in massive and mobile learning environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a system enabling students to generate and share context-aware quiz questions for peers will enhance their epistemic agency, deepen conceptual understanding, and improve learning outcomes more effectively than traditional teacher-provided assessments.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a web/mobile platform where students generate multiple-choice questions tagged with specific learning objectives and contextual cues (e.g., linking to a specific AR simulation moment or textbook diagram). The system will use a combination of natural language processing (e.g., pre-2026 BERT-based models) for initial question quality screening and a peer-rating algorithm to surface high-quality items. A quasi-experimental design will compare intervention and control groups in undergraduate science courses, measuring outcomes via pre/post content tests, surveys on self-efficacy and epistemic beliefs, and analysis of system log data on student behavior patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, technologically-supported pedagogical model for assessment that empowers students. It will provide empirical evidence on how student-generated questioning influences epistemic cognition and science learning self-efficacy, offering practical design principles for overcoming barriers to active learning in technology-integrated instruction.
proposals.novelty: While peer instruction exists, inverting the entire question-generation process to students and systematically supporting it with context-aware technology to scaffold epistemic practices is a novel synthesis. It moves beyond using technology for delivery to using it for epistemic co-construction.

proposals.title: Bridging the Epistemic Gap: A Study of Teacher Beliefs and Student Learning Efficacy in Mobile-LMS Integrated Science Assessment
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, the shift to online and blended learning highlighted significant barriers to technology integration, including fragmented tools that increase teacher workload and obscure student learning patterns. Connecting mobile assessment tools to institutional Learning Management Systems (LMS) is a practical challenge with epistemic implications, as seamless data flow could transform how teachers perceive and utilize formative data, and how students track their knowledge construction in science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a technically robust integration of a mobile science assessment tool with a mainstream LMS (e.g., Moodle, Canvas) will reduce teachers' perceived barriers to technology use and, by providing unified activity tracking, enhance undergraduate science students' self-efficacy and productive learning behaviors.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a middleware architecture using RESTful APIs and the IMS Learning Tools Interoperability (LTI) Advantage standard to connect an existing mobile assessment tool (e.g., for AR-assisted science activities) to an institutional LMS. The system will automate gradebook updates and log detailed activity sequences. Using a mixed-methods design, we will measure in-service science teachers' conceptions of integration barriers (survey, interviews) and undergraduate students' science learning self-efficacy and behavior patterns (pre/post surveys, learning analytics from the integrated logs) in a quasi-experimental study.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a validated technical framework for mobile-LMS integration and empirical evidence on how such integration influences two key actors: teachers' epistemic beliefs about assessment technology and students' cognitive and affective outcomes. It moves beyond describing barriers to testing a design-based solution within authentic learning ecologies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Tsai and others has separately studied barriers to mobile technology and online learning environments, this study uniquely investigates the epistemic and behavioral effects of *bridging* these systems, treating the integration itself as an intervention that modifies the learning-technology ecosystem.

proposals.title: A Longitudinal Study of Epistemic Engagement and Knowledge Retention: Comparing Mobile Outdoor Authentic Assessments with Traditional Indoor Tests in Science Education
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including my own, has identified barriers to effective technology-integrated instruction and highlighted the potential of mobile and augmented reality tools to influence cognitive load and self-efficacy. However, there is a critical gap in understanding the long-term epistemic and cognitive impacts of different assessment modalities. The shift towards more authentic, situated learning experiences necessitates a rigorous, longitudinal comparison of how knowledge is retained when assessed in contextually rich, outdoor mobile environments versus conventional indoor settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that science knowledge assessed through mobile, outdoor authentic tasks will demonstrate superior long-term retention and deeper epistemic understanding compared to knowledge assessed via traditional indoor tests, due to enhanced contextual encoding and reduced retrieval barriers.
proposals.method: This multi-year study will employ a mixed-methods, quasi-experimental design with undergraduate science students. Participants will be assigned to either a mobile outdoor assessment condition (using geolocated tasks and AR via smartphones/tablets) or a traditional indoor written assessment condition. We will measure knowledge retention at intervals (1 week, 3 months, 1 year) using standardized tests and concept mapping. Epistemic beliefs, cognitive load, and self-efficacy will be tracked via validated surveys (e.g., Epistemic Beliefs Inventory, NASA-TLX). Data analysis will use repeated-measures ANOVA for quantitative data and thematic analysis for interview data, with structural equation modeling to explore relationships between epistemic factors, cognitive load, and retention outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust empirical evidence on the long-term learning effectiveness of mobile-integrated, authentic assessment, directly addressing a gap in the literature on sustainable educational technology integration. It will contribute a nuanced model linking assessment design, epistemic engagement, and knowledge durability, offering practical insights for instructional and assessment design in science education.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares immediate learning outcomes, this study's longitudinal focus on knowledge retention over years, combined with its explicit investigation of the mediating role of epistemic beliefs and cognitive load in an outdoor mobile context, represents a novel and critical extension of existing research on technology-enhanced learning.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of the Epistemic Emotion and Metacognitive Regulation Scale (EEMRS) for Science Learning Contexts
proposals.motivation: Epistemic emotions (e.g., curiosity, confusion) and metacognitive processes are critical for deep learning in science, yet they are predominantly measured with qualitative or open-ended instruments, limiting large-scale quantitative analysis. Pre-2026, the surge in technology-integrated and online science education, as seen in Tsai's recent work on AR and distance education, creates a pressing need for efficient, scalable assessment tools to understand student experiences and barriers. Without robust close-ended measures, it is difficult to systematically investigate how these constructs relate to self-efficacy, cognitive load, and learning outcomes in diverse instructional settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-dimensional, close-ended scale can reliably and validly measure distinct facets of epistemic emotions and metacognitive regulation in science learning. Furthermore, we hypothesize that scores on this scale will significantly predict students' science learning self-efficacy and their patterns of engagement with technology-assisted learning materials.
proposals.method: The study will employ a sequential mixed-methods design. First, a literature review and expert interviews will generate an initial item pool for epistemic emotions (e.g., surprise, curiosity, confusion) and metacognitive regulation (planning, monitoring, evaluating). Second, we will conduct exploratory factor analysis (EFA) on data from 300+ undergraduate science students to refine the factor structure. Third, confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) with a separate sample will validate the model fit. Convergent and discriminant validity will be tested against established measures of achievement emotions (AEQ) and metacognitive awareness (MAI). The analysis will use structural equation modeling (SEM) in Mplus to test predictive relationships with self-efficacy and learning behavior logs.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the field with a rigorously validated, quantitative instrument for assessing key epistemic and metacognitive constructs, enabling larger-scale and more efficient research. It will contribute to a more nuanced understanding of the psychological mechanisms underlying learning in technology-enhanced science environments, directly informing the design of interventions to support students' epistemic engagement and self-regulation.
proposals.novelty: While scales exist for general achievement emotions and metacognition, this instrument is novel in its specific integration of *epistemic* emotions with concurrent metacognitive regulation, tailored for the context of science learning with digital tools, a gap highlighted in Tsai's prior work on cognitive load and AR.

proposals.title: Interacting Epistemic Pathways: A Multi-Construct Study of Teacher Beliefs and Student Learning in Technology-Enhanced Science Inquiry
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, research on epistemic cognition in science education often isolates constructs like personal beliefs (personal epistemology), knowledge-building practices (productive epistemology), or classroom norms (practical epistemology). This fragmentation limits understanding of how these layers interact to shape learning, particularly in technology-rich environments where such interactions are pronounced. A holistic investigation is crucial to design more effective instructional technology and teacher professional development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that alignment between a teacher's personal epistemology, the practical epistemology they foster in the classroom, and the productive epistemology demanded by a technology-enhanced inquiry task will positively predict students' cognitive engagement, self-efficacy, and conceptual understanding in science.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods design-based research study with secondary school science teachers and their students. Quantitative measures will include validated surveys for personal epistemology (e.g., Epistemic Beliefs Inventory), practical epistemology (classroom observation protocols coded for epistemic discourse), and productive epistemology (analysis of student artifacts using a knowledge-building portfolio rubric). We will employ structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the hypothesized pathways between these constructs and student outcomes (cognitive load, self-efficacy, and pre/post-test gains). Concurrently, qualitative data from teacher interviews and student focus groups will provide explanatory depth on epistemic barriers and alignment.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, integrated model of epistemic influence in technology-enhanced learning, moving beyond single-construct accounts. It will offer concrete design principles for aligning epistemic dimensions in educational technology and teacher training, directly addressing known barriers to effective technology integration highlighted in the researcher's prior work.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in simultaneously modeling the triadic interaction of personal, productive, and practical epistemologies within a single intervention study, and in linking this epistemic alignment directly to student cognitive and affective outcomes in a real-world, technology-assisted science learning context.

proposals.title: Unpacking Collaborative Epistemic Practices in AR-Supported Science Learning: A Video-Based Interaction Analysis
proposals.motivation: The integration of Augmented Reality (AR) in science education, as studied by Tsai, creates novel, embodied learning contexts. However, pre-2026 research often relies on self-reports or outcome measures, leaving the 'black box' of how students and teachers collaboratively construct knowledge in these environments poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to design effective pedagogical scaffolds that move beyond technological barriers to foster deep, collaborative scientific reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, observable patterns of verbal and gestural interaction in AR-assisted collaborative tasks will correlate with the quality of shared epistemic artifacts (e.g., models, explanations) produced by student groups. Furthermore, we hypothesize that teachers' real-time interventions will significantly shape these interaction patterns and epistemic outcomes.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-layered video-based interaction analysis, drawing on established CSCL frameworks like the 'Epistemic Network Analysis' (ENA) and 'Conversational Analysis'. Video data of small groups (n=15) and their teachers during AR-based science inquiry tasks will be collected. Coding schemes will be developed to capture epistemic practices (e.g., co-constructing explanations, managing evidence), multimodal interactions (gestures, gaze, object manipulation), and teacher moves. ENA will be used to model and statistically compare the network structure of these coded practices across high- and low-performing groups, identifying critical interactional sequences.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a fine-grained, process-oriented account of how collaborative knowledge building unfolds in immersive learning environments, directly linking interaction patterns to epistemic outcomes. It will provide educators and designers with an empirically grounded framework for recognizing and supporting productive collaborative practices in technology-integrated science classrooms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying rigorous CSCL-derived interaction analysis, specifically ENA, to the multimodal, spatially complex context of AR-assisted science learning—a context Tsai has studied via cognitive load and self-efficacy but not through this detailed epistemic practice lens. This bridges his work on technology barriers with a deeper analysis of the collaborative knowledge processes they aim to enable.

proposals.title: Epistemic Beliefs and Social Network Structures in K-12 Teacher Professional Development: A Barrier Analysis for Technology Integration
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Tsai's own work, identifies teacher beliefs and contextual barriers as critical to technology integration in instruction. However, studies on social networks in educational settings have predominantly focused on university contexts or student collaboration, neglecting the analysis of informal support networks among K-12 teachers. Understanding the structure and epistemic characteristics of these networks is crucial for designing effective professional development that overcomes well-documented barriers to mobile and digital tool adoption.
proposals.hypothesis: The density and centrality of a K-12 teacher's informal social network for instructional support are significantly predicted by their epistemic beliefs about technology-integrated learning, and these network characteristics, in turn, mediate the perceived barriers to implementing technology in the classroom.
proposals.method: The study will employ a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design. First, a large-scale survey of K-12 science teachers will measure their epistemic beliefs about technology (using a validated Likert-scale instrument), map their ego-centric instructional support networks (using name-generator questions), and assess perceived barriers (scaled from Tsai's prior work). Social network analysis (SNA) metrics (e.g., density, betweenness centrality) will be computed using UCINET. Quantitative analysis will use structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the mediation hypothesis. Subsequently, qualitative interviews with purposively selected teachers from distinct network positions will provide deeper insight into the epistemic nature of network interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the literature on teacher beliefs and barriers by formally modeling the social context as a mediating variable. It will provide a novel, network-aware framework for diagnosing systemic challenges in professional development. The findings will offer concrete design principles for interventions that leverage or reshape teacher networks to foster more effective technology integration in K-12 science education.
proposals.novelty: While teacher beliefs and barriers are well-studied, this proposal uniquely integrates epistemic belief constructs with formal social network analysis to explain variation in barrier perception within the under-researched context of K-12 teacher communities, moving beyond individualistic or purely university-focused models.

proposals.title: Epistemic and Social Boundaries: A Mixed-Methods Study of Student Privacy Concerns in Social Media-Integrated Science Learning
proposals.motivation: The shift to online and technology-integrated learning, accelerated by the COVID-19 pandemic, has blurred the lines between academic and personal digital spaces. Instructors increasingly use platforms like Facebook or Discord for course communication and collaboration, yet pre-2026 research on barriers to technology integration has largely focused on teacher perspectives and technical challenges, neglecting student perceptions of privacy and professional boundary management in these contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: Students' perceived epistemic barriers to knowledge construction in social media-integrated courses are significantly influenced by their concerns over privacy and the erosion of student-teacher boundaries. We hypothesize that these concerns will cluster into distinct learner profiles that predict self-efficacy and engagement behavior patterns.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design. First, a survey will be administered to undergraduate science students, adapting established scales on technology acceptance, privacy concerns, and self-efficacy, and including novel items on boundary perceptions. Latent Profile Analysis (LPA) will be used to identify distinct student profiles based on survey responses. Subsequently, purposive sampling from each profile will inform in-depth phenomenological interviews to explore the epistemic and social reasoning behind their concerns. Thematic analysis of interview data will explain the quantitative profiles.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel, student-centered framework for understanding a key socio-epistemic barrier to digital learning. It will extend Tsai's prior work on learner profiles and cognitive load by mapping profiles of privacy and boundary concerns. The findings will provide evidence-based design principles for instructors and educational technologists to implement social tools in ways that respect student autonomy and support epistemic engagement.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Tsai and others has examined barriers from the teacher's perspective or focused on cognitive load, this study uniquely investigates the under-explored intersection of student privacy, professional boundaries, and epistemic engagement, applying a profiling methodology to this social-epistemic problem.

proposals.title: Developing and Validating the Web-Specific Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (Web-TPACK) Instrument for Science Teachers
proposals.motivation: The shift to massive distance and online learning environments, accelerated pre-2026, has made the World Wide Web a fundamental educational infrastructure. However, existing TPACK instruments are generic, failing to capture the specific technological knowledge required to effectively leverage the Web's unique affordances (e.g., information literacy, open-source tools, hypermedia integration) and barriers for science teaching. Without a domain-specific measure, it is impossible to diagnose teachers' precise professional development needs or design targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a validated, multi-dimensional instrument measuring Web-specific TPACK (Web-TPACK) can reliably distinguish between teachers' knowledge components and that these components will differentially predict their self-efficacy and perceived barriers to implementing Web-based science instruction.
proposals.method: Following established scale development procedures, we will: 1) Conduct a literature review and interviews with in-service science teachers to generate an initial item pool for Web-TK, Web-TPK, Web-TCK, and integrated Web-TPACK. 2) Employ exploratory factor analysis (EFA) with data from 200+ teachers to refine the factor structure. 3) Validate the structure using confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) with a separate sample. 4) Establish construct validity by correlating Web-TPACK subscales with established measures of technology integration barriers, self-efficacy, and observed behavior patterns in simulated Web-based lesson design tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated instrument to diagnose science teachers' knowledge for Web-integrated instruction, moving beyond generic technology measures. It will contribute to the epistemic understanding of TPACK as a context-dependent construct and offer a concrete tool for designing precise professional development to overcome the specific barriers identified in Tsai's prior work on mobile and online learning.
proposals.novelty: While TPACK instruments exist, none are specialized for the World Wide Web as a distinct technological platform with its own pedagogical and content-specific implications for science education. This instrument explicitly links TPACK measurement to the specific barriers and affordances of the Web environment.

proposals.title: Designing for Epistemic Engagement: A Design-Based Research Study on Social Network Learning Activities in Undergraduate Science Education
proposals.motivation: The shift to massive and online learning environments, accelerated pre-2026, has highlighted challenges in fostering meaningful knowledge co-construction and epistemic engagement among students. While social network platforms offer potential for collaborative learning, their integration into science education often fails to address teachers' and students' epistemic beliefs and the cognitive barriers to technology use, leading to superficial interactions rather than deep learning.
proposals.hypothesis: Iterative design cycles that explicitly target the alignment of activity structure, technological affordances, and participants' epistemic beliefs will yield a set of design principles for social network learning activities that measurably enhance students' collaborative knowledge-building practices and science learning self-efficacy.
proposals.method: This project will employ a three-cycle Design-Based Research (DBR) methodology in an undergraduate science course. Each cycle involves: (1) Contextual analysis of student and teacher epistemic beliefs via surveys and interviews, informed by Tsai's prior work. (2) Co-design of a social network learning activity (e.g., a collaborative hypothesis refinement task on a platform like Slack or a custom forum) with instructors. (3) Implementation and mixed-methods evaluation using learning analytics (e.g., social network analysis of interaction patterns, linguistic analysis of discourse for epistemic quality), cognitive load and self-efficacy scales, and participant reflection. Findings from each cycle will inform the redesign for the next.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a theoretically grounded and empirically tested set of design principles for social network learning, specifically addressing the intersection of technology, epistemology, and collaboration. It will advance the field by providing a replicable DBR model for overcoming identified barriers to technology-integrated instruction and moving beyond descriptive studies of behavior patterns to intervention-based research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating the analysis of epistemic beliefs—a core theme in Tsai's work—as a central driver of the DBR design process for social learning activities, rather than treating it merely as an outcome variable. This approach directly links teachers' conceptions of barriers to the iterative design of solutions.

proposals.title: Unveiling Epistemic Engagement: A Discourse Analysis of Student Interactions in Asynchronous Online Science Learning Environments
proposals.motivation: The shift to massive and distance education, accelerated pre-2026, has amplified reliance on text-based student discussions for learning. However, existing analytics often focus on surface-level participation metrics, failing to capture the depth of knowledge construction and epistemic reasoning within these interactions. Grounded in Tsai's prior work on barriers to technology-integrated instruction and online learning challenges, this study addresses the critical need to understand the qualitative nature of student discourse to inform better pedagogical design and support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying systematic linguistic discourse analysis to student posts will reveal distinct, empirically identifiable patterns of epistemic engagement (e.g., explanatory, exploratory, confirmatory) that are more predictive of learning outcomes and self-efficacy than traditional quantitative measures of forum activity.
proposals.method: The study will employ a mixed-methods discourse analysis framework. First, student posts and comment threads from an existing online undergraduate science course dataset will be collected. Analysis will proceed in two phases: (1) A computational linguistics phase using pre-2026 natural language processing tools (e.g., LIWC dictionaries, Latent Dirichlet Allocation for topic modeling) to identify lexical and thematic features. (2) A manual, fine-grained phase applying established discourse analysis schemes (e.g., a coding framework for epistemic cognition verbs, claim-evidence-reasoning structures, and interactive cohesion) to a stratified sample. These discourse patterns will be statistically correlated with pre-existing measures of science learning self-efficacy and final course grades.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel analytical framework for diagnosing the quality of epistemic discourse in online science learning. It will provide educators with evidence-based discourse markers to identify students struggling with deep engagement and offer concrete design principles for crafting discussion prompts that foster higher-order knowledge construction, moving beyond simplistic 'post-and-reply' requirements.
proposals.novelty: While discourse analysis is established in linguistics, its systematic application to correlate specific epistemic discourse features with learning self-efficacy and outcomes in asynchronous online science contexts is novel. It bridges Tsai's expertise in technology-integrated learning barriers with a deep, qualitative investigation of the knowledge-building process itself.

proposals.title: Scaffolding Epistemic Engagement in MOOCs: A Social Network-Informed Design to Foster Knowledge Co-construction at Scale
proposals.motivation: Massive Open Online Courses (MOOCs) suffer from high dropout rates and often fail to foster meaningful collaborative knowledge building, despite their potential for large-scale education. Pre-2026 research identifies social isolation and a lack of structured peer interaction as key barriers to engagement and deep learning in these environments. This study addresses the critical challenge of scaling effective collaborative learning principles, informed by social network theory, to support thousands of participants.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a MOOC platform design that algorithmically structures large participant cohorts into dynamic, overlapping sub-communities based on epistemic contributions and interaction patterns will lead to higher perceived social presence, greater cognitive engagement, and improved learning outcomes compared to standard forum-based MOOC designs.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a prototype MOOC module integrating a backend algorithm that uses learner interaction data (post content, replies, upvotes) to dynamically form and suggest 'epistemic clusters'. These are temporary, topic-focused discussion groups that bridge learners with complementary knowledge or questions. The design will be tested in a quasi-experimental study comparing the intervention platform with a control platform using a traditional linear discussion forum. Data collection will include system logs, pre/post surveys on self-efficacy and epistemic beliefs, and content analysis of discourse quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable instructional design framework for MOOCs that translates social network analysis principles into actionable pedagogical tools. It will provide empirical evidence on how to reduce barriers to social learning in massive settings and offer new insights into the relationship between designed social structures and students' epistemic engagement in online science learning.
proposals.novelty: While social network analysis has been used to *describe* MOOC interactions, this study proactively *uses* its principles to *design* the learning environment's social architecture. It moves beyond static forums or small-group fixes by creating adaptive, large-scale social structures informed by real-time epistemic behavior.

proposals.title: Flipping the Script: Examining Epistemic Agency and Learning Outcomes When Students Design Peer Social Network Activities in Science Education
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, research on technology-integrated instruction, including social networks for learning, predominantly focuses on teacher-led design, often identifying teacher beliefs and institutional barriers as key challenges. This overlooks the potential of student epistemic agency. Concurrently, studies on cognitive load and self-efficacy in augmented reality highlight the importance of learner-centered design. This study addresses the gap by investigating if shifting design authority to students can overcome traditional barriers and foster deeper engagement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that students who design and moderate social network-based learning activities for their peers will demonstrate greater science learning self-efficacy, more sophisticated epistemic beliefs about knowledge construction, and exhibit more coherent behavioral patterns in online discourse compared to students in teacher-designed network activities.
proposals.method: The study will employ a mixed-methods, design-based research approach. Undergraduate science students will be assigned to design teams using a structured, scaffolded design protocol (based on pre-2026 learning design frameworks) to create peer discussion prompts and moderation rules for a closed social network platform (e.g., a customized instance of Discourse or Slack). Behavioral sequence analysis and epistemic network analysis will be applied to discourse logs to quantify interaction patterns. Pre/post surveys will measure changes in self-efficacy and epistemic beliefs, complemented by focus group interviews on the designers' experiences.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel model of 'student-led network design' to the learning sciences, providing empirical evidence on its impact on epistemic development and self-regulation. It will also offer practical design principles for leveraging student agency to mitigate known barriers to technology integration identified in teacher-centric models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically flipping the design perspective from teacher to student within social network learning, directly connecting student-led design activity to measurable changes in epistemic beliefs and peer interaction patterns, an approach not prominent in pre-2026 literature on barriers and technology integration.

proposals.title: Mapping the Epistemic Networks of Self-Directed Teacher Learning: How Informal Communities Foster Integrated Knowledge for Technology-Enhanced Science Instruction
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, teacher professional development (PD) for technology integration often relies on structured, formal programmes, which can be resource-intensive and misaligned with teachers' immediate needs and contexts. This study addresses a critical gap by investigating how in-service science teachers develop the integrated Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (TPACK) framework through informal, self-directed learning networks, a pervasive but under-researched phenomenon accelerated by online platforms and social media.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that teachers' participation in informal, online professional learning networks (PLNs) is positively associated with the development of more integrated and sophisticated TPACK profiles, and that these epistemic gains are mediated by specific types of knowledge-sharing interactions and the reduction of perceived barriers to technology integration identified in teachers' discourse.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods design study will recruit 50-60 in-service science teachers active in informal online communities (e.g., subject-specific subreddits, Facebook groups, Twitter/X hashtags). Data collection will involve: (1) administering pre- and post-surveys measuring TPACK and self-efficacy; (2) using Epistemic Network Analysis (ENA) to model the structure and evolution of teachers' integrated knowledge from their online discourse over six months; and (3) conducting semi-structured interviews to explore perceived barriers and enablers. Quantitative analysis will employ mediation models, while ENA will visualize and statistically compare knowledge network structures between high- and low-gain participants.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, data-rich model of how integrated teacher knowledge develops organically outside formal PD structures. It will provide empirical evidence for the value of informal PLNs and offer design principles for leveraging these networks to complement traditional PD, ultimately supporting more scalable and responsive models of teacher learning in the digital age.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying Epistemic Network Analysis—a method typically used to study student reasoning—to model the complex, integrated knowledge structures of teachers within informal learning ecosystems, directly challenging the assumption that structured intervention is the primary conduit for professional growth.

proposals.title: Scaling Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge: A Network Analysis of Knowledge Integration and Epistemic Barriers in a Nationwide Online Teacher Community
proposals.motivation: The rapid shift to technology-integrated and distance education, accelerated by global events, has exposed significant gaps in teachers' TPACK, creating a critical need for scalable professional development. Existing models are often resource-intensive and localized, failing to address the massive, systemic need. This study investigates whether massive, open online professional learning communities (MOPLCs) can provide a viable, sustainable mechanism for nationwide TPACK scaling, directly addressing the barriers and challenges identified in prior research on technology integration and distance education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that participation in a structured, nationwide MOPLC will lead to measurable growth in teachers' integrated TPACK, and that this growth will be mediated by the reduction of specific epistemic barriers (e.g., beliefs about technology's role) and facilitated by particular patterns of social knowledge construction within the community network.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a year-long, nationwide MOPLC for in-service science teachers, using a mixed-methods, design-based research approach. Quantitative data will include pre/post surveys measuring TPACK components, self-efficacy, and epistemic beliefs, analyzed using structural equation modeling to test mediation. Concurrently, we will employ computational ethnography: all digital interactions (forum posts, resource shares, co-created lesson plans) will be timestamped and analyzed using social network analysis (SNA) algorithms (e.g., community detection, centrality measures) and epistemic network analysis (ENA) to model the structure and content of knowledge integration. Qualitative analysis of discussion threads will provide context for the network patterns.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a validated, scalable model for TPACK development at a systems level. It will generate new theory on how knowledge integrates across large, distributed networks of practitioners, specifically identifying the social and epistemic mechanisms that enable or hinder TPACK growth. The findings will provide actionable design principles for creating effective large-scale online professional learning ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While online communities for teachers exist, this study uniquely combines TPACK, epistemic barriers, and computational network analysis to model the *process* of knowledge scaling at a massive level, moving beyond self-report to trace the actual flow and integration of technological, pedagogical, and content knowledge within a nationwide digital social system.

proposals.title: Disentangling Confidence from Competence: A Multi-Method Investigation of Preservice Teachers' TPACK Self-Efficacy and Applied Performance
proposals.motivation: Effective technology integration in classrooms hinges on teachers' Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (TPACK). Pre-2026 research, including Tsai's work on teacher barriers, heavily relies on self-reported TPACK confidence. However, self-perception is a known poor proxy for actual competence. If a significant disconnect exists, teacher education programs may be misled by self-efficacy surveys, failing to address critical skill gaps that undermine classroom technology use.
proposals.hypothesis: Preservice teachers' self-reported TPACK confidence will show a weak or non-linear correlation with their demonstrated ability to design and justify a technology-integrated science lesson plan. Furthermore, specific epistemic beliefs about the nature of knowledge in science will moderate this relationship.
proposals.method: A mixed-methods design will be employed. First, a validated TPACK self-efficacy survey and an epistemic beliefs questionnaire will be administered to N=200+ preservice science teachers. Second, participants will complete a performance-based assessment: designing a science lesson plan integrating a specific AR simulation (e.g., PhET), with rationale. Two expert raters will score plans using a validated TPACK-in-Action rubric. Correlation and regression analyses (e.g., using SPSS or R) will test the relationship between self-efficacy and performance scores. A cluster analysis (e.g., k-means) will identify distinct profiles of high/low confidence and competence. Qualitative content analysis of lesson plan rationales will explore epistemic justifications for technology use.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence quantifying the confidence-competence gap in TPACK, moving the field beyond reliance on self-report. It will yield a validated performance task for assessing applied TPACK. The findings will inform teacher educators on how to tailor training interventions, potentially shifting focus from boosting confidence to building observable, epistemically grounded design skills.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines TPACK beliefs and barriers separately, this study directly contrasts self-perception with observed performance in a design task. It innovatively integrates epistemic belief frameworks to explain the disconnect, linking teachers' views on science knowledge to their ability to justify technology integration, a nuance not fully explored in pre-2026 TPACK literature.

proposals.title: From Course to Classroom: A Longitudinal Study of Preservice Teachers' TPACK Trajectories and the Role of Epistemic Beliefs and Contextual Barriers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge (TPACK) often measures development within single courses, leaving a critical gap in understanding how these competencies evolve and are applied in authentic teaching contexts. Given the documented barriers to technology integration and the shift towards online/distance learning, it is essential to track whether TPACK gained in preservice training is sustained and adapted during the crucial transition into professional practice.
proposals.hypothesis: Preservice teachers' TPACK development will follow distinct longitudinal trajectories post-course, which are significantly predicted by their epistemic beliefs about knowledge and learning, and moderated by the perceived contextual barriers (technological, pedagogical, institutional) encountered during student teaching and early in-service practice.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods longitudinal design will track three cohorts of preservice teachers (N≈150) from a technology-integrated science methods course, through student teaching, and into their first two in-service years. Quantitative data will be collected via validated TPACK, epistemic beliefs, and barriers surveys at four timepoints. Latent Growth Curve Modeling (LGCM) and Growth Mixture Modeling (GMM) in Mplus will be used to model TPACK trajectories and identify distinct developmental profiles. Qualitative data from semi-structured interviews and lesson artifacts will provide explanatory depth on how beliefs and barriers shape TPACK application.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed longitudinal map of TPACK development beyond coursework, identifying critical transition points and resilience factors. It will offer teacher educators a evidence-based model for designing interventions that target epistemic beliefs and prepare teachers for real-world barriers, ultimately strengthening the pipeline of technology-proficient science teachers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines TPACK or barriers in isolation, this study uniquely integrates them within a longitudinal framework, using advanced statistical modeling (GMM) to reveal heterogeneous developmental pathways and testing the pre-2026 theoretical proposition that epistemic beliefs are a foundational driver of TPACK enactment.

ideas.description: This research first employs ethnographic studies, learning analytics, and expert interviews to systematically identify and prioritize concrete, high-impact pedagogical failures and unmet needs in target learning contexts. It then develops a structured evaluation framework to assess which emerging technologies (e.g., AR, AI, IoT) are most pragmatically suited to address each validated need, based on criteria of cognitive fit, scalability, and accessibility. The output is a practical, evidence-based decision matrix and prototyping methodology that guides educators and developers in deploying technology only where it demonstrably improves a defined learning process or outcome.

ideas.title: Asynchronous State-Signaling for Adaptive Learning Environments
ideas.description: This research designs learning environments that adapt effectively without real-time sensing by leveraging deliberate, asynchronous student signaling. The system integrates periodic, structured opportunities for learners to self-report their understanding, confidence, and context, which then triggers pre-computed adaptive responses in the instructional material and problem sequencing. This approach maintains learner agency and privacy while overcoming the rigidity of purely static designs, ensuring the environment responds to critical shifts in student state. The core investigation compares the efficacy and engagement of this signaling-based adaptation against both static environments and fully sensor-driven real-time systems.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Learning Analytics: A Participatory Framework for Inferring Learning Context from Multimodal Data
ideas.description: This research develops a participatory, context-sensitive framework for interpreting passive multimodal data (e.g., device use, location, ambient audio) in learning environments, explicitly avoiding clinical analogies. Instead of diagnosing states, the system infers situational 'learning context'—such as focused study, collaborative work, or procedural difficulty—by fusing passive data with periodic, learner-initiated micro-surveys that provide ground truth. The analytical models and output metrics are co-designed with educators and students to ensure they reflect meaningful educational constructs and support pedagogical intervention. The system includes an ethical governance layer that mandates transparency, gives learners control over data sharing, and directs outputs solely toward providing timely, contextual academic support and resource recommendations.

The study will identify key adoption barriers, critical success factors, and likely diffusion pathways for these technologies across different educational sectors and adopter categories. This enables the development of evidence-based roadmaps and intervention strategies to facilitate the effective integration of AI-AR tools, optimizing their implementation for enhanced learning outcomes.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Edge Effects to Design Productive Boundaries in Blended Learning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that online and real-world learning should be seamlessly integrated. Drawing an analogy from Landscape Ecology's management of 'edge effects' between ecosystems, the research reconceptualizes the boundary as a designed transitional zone with unique pedagogical value. The analogous solution—intentionally structuring buffer zones and graded transitions to regulate interactions—is adapted to create specific 'boundary interventions' in learning design. These interventions, such as context-switching rituals or dual-realm objects, are not meant to erase the boundary but to make it a site of productive friction, fostering learner metacognition and clarifying the distinct affordances of digital and physical learning spaces.

ideas.title: A Participatory Meta-Framework for Contextually Adaptive Smart Learning Environments
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a participatory meta-framework and co-design toolkit for creating contextually adaptive smart learning environments (SLEs). Instead of a single design framework, it produces a process model and a set of core, evidence-based principles that are dynamically adapted through structured collaboration with local stakeholders in diverse global settings. The validation involves iterative cycles of co-design, prototype implementation, and impact assessment across a strategically selected portfolio of contrasting educational contexts (e.g., differing in culture, resource level, and pedagogical tradition). The ultimate output is a robust, inclusive methodology for generating effective, locally owned SLE designs that integrate online and real-world activities.

ideas.title: Multimodal, Contextually-Adaptive Learning Environments (MCALE) using Immersive Sensing
ideas.description: This research investigates the design of Multimodal, Contextually-Adaptive Learning Environments (MCALE) that leverage emerging immersive technologies like Augmented Reality as dual-purpose tools for both content delivery and rich data capture. The core innovation is using the AR system's sensors (e.g., cameras, eye-tracking, motion sensors) to continuously infer a wide array of student factors—including cognitive load, emotional engagement, situational interest, and physical context—during authentic learning tasks. By integrating this multimodal data stream with advanced learner models, the system can dynamically adapt instructional scaffolding, content presentation, and task complexity in real-time. This creates a deeply personalized and responsive learning experience that is fundamentally shaped by the holistic state of the learner within their specific environment.

ideas.title: Causally-Grounded, Auditable Learning System Design
ideas.description: This research designs learning systems that move beyond factor correlation by embedding a causal inference framework into their core architecture. The system explicitly models hypothesized causal relationships between student traits, contextual factors, and learning outcomes, testing these models through controlled experiments and observational data analysis. This approach ensures integrated factors have a justified, evidence-based mechanistic link to pedagogical adaptations, dramatically improving robustness and fairness. The result is an adaptive learning system whose recommendations are transparent, derived from testable causal theories, and whose potential biases can be traced and corrected within the causal model.

ideas.title: Longitudinal and Equity-Centric Validation of Smart Learning Features in Complex Educational Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond one-off validation studies to implement a long-term, embedded research-practice partnership. It employs a multi-cohort longitudinal design to track the sustained impact of smart learning features on cognitive, metacognitive, and non-cognitive outcomes. The core innovation is its embedded equity audit, which continuously analyzes differential effects across demographic and socioeconomic subgroups to detect and address algorithmic bias or access disparities. The system integrates real-time feedback loops with educators, allowing for the iterative refinement of features based on both empirical data and practitioner wisdom within authentic, complex school environments.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Activity Theory and Agent-Based Modeling Framework for the Predictive Analysis and Design of Smart Learning Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a novel hybrid framework that combines the descriptive, human-centered lens of Activity Theory with the formal, computational power of Agent-Based Modeling (ABM). The Activity Theory model is used to structure the key elements and contradictions within a smart learning environment. These elements are then operationalized into an ABM simulation where agents (learners, instructors) interact according to the rules, tools, and divisions of labor defined by the theory. This allows researchers and designers to move beyond static analysis, enabling them to simulate the dynamic emergence of systemic outcomes, test the impact of potential design interventions, and predict how contradictions might evolve or be resolved over time.

ideas.title: Orchestration-Aware Smart Learning: A Teacher-in-the-Loop Framework for Diagnosing and Mitigating Systemic Learning Issues
ideas.description: This research direction develops smart learning systems that explicitly bridge the gap between identifying macro-level research issues and providing micro-level teacher support. It moves beyond diagnosing learning problems to co-designing, with teachers, orchestration tools that offer predictive insights and intervention strategies for issues like inequitable participation or curriculum pacing mismatches across physical and digital spaces. The core novelty is a feedback loop where teacher orchestration actions, informed by system diagnostics, themselves generate new data to refine the understanding of the underlying pedagogical issues, creating a scalable model for evidence-informed teaching practice.

ideas.title: Institutionally-Grounded Co-Design: Integrating Ethnography and Value Negotiation for Viable Smart Learning Environments
ideas.description: This approach embeds participatory design within a deeper analysis of the school's institutional reality. It first employs ethnographic methods to understand the political, economic, and social constraints teachers and students operate within. Then, using value-sensitive design frameworks, it facilitates structured workshops where stakeholders not only design technological features but also collaboratively model the necessary shifts in policy, professional development, and resource distribution. The outcome is a co-created implementation blueprint for a smart learning environment that is technically sound, pedagogically aligned, and politically viable within the specific institutional context.

ideas.title: Triadic: Environment adaptation governed by normative pedagogical agents
ideas.description: This system creates a smart learning environment where the core adaptive engine is not directly driven by student responses. Instead, a separate normative agent, instantiated with foundational pedagogical theory and evidence-based learning progressions, acts as a governor. This agent analyzes the environment's proposed adaptations and the student's behavioral data, approving only those changes that align with principled learning objectives and discourage shallow optimization. The result is an environment that learns to strategically provoke productive struggle, introduce desirable difficulties, and diversify its interventions based on a robust model of learning, not just engagement.

ideas.title: Retrospective Analysis Learning Environments (RALE): Using Delayed, Aggregated Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that effective adaptive learning requires real-time sensing of student status. Drawing an analogy from epidemiology, which uses delayed, aggregated data for syndromic surveillance and cohort studies to guide public health decisions, we propose Retrospective Analysis Learning Environments (RALE). The analogous solution is adapted by designing systems that collect digital trace data from learning activities (e.g., login times, quiz attempts, discussion contributions) in batches—say, daily or weekly. This aggregated data is then analyzed using statistical and machine learning models to reconstruct learning journeys, identify effective or ineffective sequences, and detect signs of confusion or disengagement post-hoc. The environment or instructor uses these insights not for immediate intervention, but to strategically redesign the next module, provide targeted feedback, or form peer groups, creating a powerful, non-invasive, and scalable feedback loop that respects student privacy and reduces dependency on continuous real-time monitoring.

ideas.title: Isolate key factors for successful TPACK professional development
ideas.description: Research should disentangle which specific factors like engagement or authenticity are most critical for TPACK programme success.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Review of Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Modeling to Map and Optimize Smart Learning Environments
ideas.description: This research idea tackles smart learning issues by drawing an analogy from Ecology. In that field, scientists solve the problem of predicting where a species can thrive by building Ecological Niche Models (ENMs) that correlate species presence with environmental variables. We adapt this solution to smart learning. The adapted idea is to develop 'Learning Environment Niche Models.' These models would treat successful learning outcomes or highly engaged learner profiles as analogous to a species' presence. By analyzing data on these outcomes across a multitude of smart learning environments—characterized by variables like interface design, pedagogical approach, collaboration tools, feedback mechanisms, and access to resources—we can use machine learning to model the complex 'niche' required for success. This approach provides a predictive framework to systematically map, design, and optimize smart learning ecosystems, moving beyond trial-and-error to identify the precise conditions under which specific educational technologies and methodologies will be most effective for different learners and goals.

ideas.title: Establish government policies for lifelong learning support
ideas.description: Governments should develop policy frameworks to fund and structure technology-enabled lifelong learning initiatives.
ideas.sourceTitle: Challenges and Future Directions of Big Data and Artificial Intelligence in Education

ideas.title: Strategic Contextual Mismatch: A Scaffolded Framework for Inducing Productive Reflection
ideas.description: This research tests a hypothesis that deliberately presenting information outside its expected context can enhance deep learning, but only under specific, controlled conditions. The system first assesses a learner's mastery of a core concept, then introduces a related principle or problem in a novel and seemingly incongruent context (e.g., a physics concept in a history lesson). This mismatch is paired with guided reflection prompts and collaborative discussion tools to help learners forge new connections. The efficacy is rigorously measured by comparing transfer and retention against a control group receiving standard, contextually-aligned instruction.

ideas.title: Apply think-aloud protocols from problem-solving research
ideas.description: Collect concurrent verbal protocols during web searches to capture real-time evaluative judgments and strategy use.
ideas.sourceTitle: Information searching strategies in web-based science learning: the role of internet self-efficacy

ideas.title: Adapting Clinical Trial Phases for Validating Smart Learning Features
ideas.description: This idea adapts the rigorous, phased validation framework from pharmaceutical clinical trials to the empirical testing of smart learning features and criteria. In pharmaceutical development, a candidate drug undergoes sequential phases: Phase I (small-scale safety), Phase II (controlled efficacy), Phase III (large-scale effectiveness), and Phase IV (post-market surveillance). The adapted research idea proposes applying this structured, staged approach to validate smart learning innovations. This would involve Phase I studies testing feature usability and potential risks in lab classrooms, Phase II randomized controlled trials measuring learning efficacy against defined criteria, Phase III large-scale implementations across diverse real educational settings to assess general effectiveness, and Phase IV longitudinal studies monitoring long-term educational outcomes and systemic integration. This framework ensures a comprehensive, evidence-based pathway from theoretical feature design to proven, scalable educational practice.

ideas.title: Invert the 'right place, right time' paradigm
ideas.description: Explore if deliberately providing information at the 'wrong' time or place could enhance learning through reflection.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Derive course design from target TPACK profiles

ideas.title: Challenge that more data always improves learning

ideas.title: Adopt geocaching mechanics for learning

ideas.sourceTitle: Criteria, Strategies and Research Issues of Context-Aware Ubiquitous Learning

ideas.title: Collective-Adaptive Learning Environments (CALE)
ideas.description: This research investigates smart learning environments that adapt at the group or community level by learning from how the collective student body responds to its interventions. The environment's intelligence shifts from tracking individual learning paths to detecting and fostering productive group states—such as optimal levels of collaborative debate, peer teaching, or collective flow—that emerge from student interactions. By observing and analyzing these macro-level adaptations, the system can then proactively structure tasks, form sub-groups, or adjust shared resources to catalyze beneficial collective learning conditions. This creates a feedback loop where the environment learns which communal configurations and prompts lead to superior group outcomes, thereby enhancing learning through socially scaffolded adaptation.

ideas.title: Use think-aloud protocols to assess TPACK integration
ideas.description: Apply cognitive psychology methods to capture preservice teachers' real-time reasoning while planning technology-integrated science lessons.
ideas.sourceTitle: Facilitating Preservice Teachers' Development of Technological, Pedagogical, and Content Knowledge (TPACK)

ideas.title: Dual: Environment learns from student adaptation
ideas.description: Create systems where the smart environment itself learns and adapts based on how students respond to its prompts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Research trends in mobile and ubiquitous learning: a review of publications in selected journals from 2001 to 2010

ideas.title: Dual problem: Focus on in-service teacher TPACK erosion
ideas.description: Study the factors that lead to the decay or non-use of TPACK after teachers enter the profession.
ideas.sourceTitle: Facilitating Preservice Teachers' Development of Technological, Pedagogical, and Content Knowledge (TPACK)

ideas.title: Longitudinal studies on TPACK PD's impact on student achievement
ideas.description: Future work must track how teacher TPACK professional development ultimately affects long-term student learning and achievement.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Review of Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge

ideas.title: Create teacher education programs on AI and data
ideas.description: Design professional development to train educators in using and interpreting AI-driven educational tools.
ideas.sourceTitle: Challenges and Future Directions of Big Data and Artificial Intelligence in Education

ideas.title: Robust Offline Context Reconstruction for Learning Analytics
ideas.description: This research direction applies sensor fusion techniques from robotics—such as Kalman filters and probabilistic graphical models—not for real-time detection, but to retrospectively fuse and interpret discontinuous, heterogeneous data traces (e.g., sporadic location pings, timestamps of digital interactions, ambient audio snippets, and device usage logs). By treating the learning environment as a 'sensor network' with missing and noisy data, the system builds a coherent, multi-modal narrative of the learner's context, activities, and potential cognitive states after the fact. This enables the design of learning environments and analytics that are both highly effective and privacy-preserving, as they do not require continuous monitoring, yet yield rich insights for personalization and research previously thought to require real-time sensing.

ideas.title: Scale up teacher AI-ed training via micro-credentials
ideas.description: Create a massive open online course system for global teacher education on AI tools.
ideas.sourceTitle: Challenges and Future Directions of Big Data and Artificial Intelligence in Education

ideas.title: Invert the assumption of individualized learning superiority
ideas.description: Explore when and why standardized, group-based AI instruction outperforms fully personalized pathways.
ideas.sourceTitle: Challenges and Future Directions of Big Data and Artificial Intelligence in Education

ideas.title: Multifactorial Adaptive Learning Systems (MALS) using ecological resilience modeling
ideas.description: This research proposes designing learning systems using principles adapted from ecological resilience modeling. In ecology, scientists successfully predict complex system behavior by modeling multiple interacting factors rather than isolated variables. The analogous solution involves dynamic systems approaches that account for biodiversity, environmental conditions, disturbance patterns, and spatial relationships to understand ecosystem responses. Adapted to learning systems, this approach would develop Multifactorial Adaptive Learning Systems (MALS) that integrate diverse student factors (cognitive, emotional, motivational, prior knowledge) with contextual factors (social, technological, cultural, temporal) in interactive models. These systems would use dynamic modeling to predict how factor combinations influence learning trajectories, enabling more sophisticated personalization that accounts for complex interactions rather than simple context-awareness.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Derive needed PD from student data
ideas.description: Use data on student learning difficulties with technology to inversely engineer required teacher TPACK development.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Review of Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge

ideas.title: Adopt think-aloud protocols from cognitive science

ideas.title: Adapting Precision Livestock Farming's Behavioral State Models for Student Engagement
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Precision Livestock Farming (PLF), where passive sensor data from animals is used to model behavioral states and predict outcomes. In PLF, accelerometers, audio, and video data are processed via machine learning to classify phenotypes like feeding or stress without direct observation. Adapted to education, this involves using multi-modal passive data from student devices (clickstream, camera, microphone, accelerometer) to train models that classify granular learning behavior states (e.g., focused reading, collaborative discussion, frustration) and predict learning progression or the need for support, creating a continuous, objective digital phenotyping system for student engagement and cognitive states.

ideas.title: Dual: Adaptive Learning via Homeostatic Educational Environments
ideas.description: This research adapts the homeostatic control principle from systems physiology to create self-regulating educational environments. The analogous problem—maintaining a stable internal state amidst external fluctuations—is solved in biology via continuous sensor-effector feedback loops. Here, the smart environment is designed as a homeostatic entity. It learns from real-time student adaptations (the sensor input) and uses that data to dynamically adjust its instructional prompts and supports (the effector output). The system's own learning algorithms are meta-adapted based on the longitudinal success of these interventions in maintaining student engagement and mastery, creating a dual-loop where the environment learns how best to learn from and for the student.

ideas.title: Dynamical attention is not the only mediator
ideas.description: Investigate other cognitive mediators between sensorimotor action and conceptual development beyond attentional structures.
ideas.sourceTitle: A review of using eye-tracking technology in exploring learning from 2000 to 2012

ideas.title: Transfer network analysis from sociology
