ideas.sourceTitle: Nested epistemologies: Science teachers' beliefs of teaching, learning and science

ideas.title: Apply diffusion of innovations theory to TPACK adoption
ideas.description: Use Rogers' diffusion of innovations framework to study how TPACK spreads within teacher communities.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Review of Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge

ideas.title: AI-Dictated Pedagogical Regimes: Designing Societal Adaptation to Non-Adaptive Educational AI
ideas.description: This research inverts the typical adaptive paradigm by investigating how the core architectural constraint of a powerful AI—specifically, its inability to perform real-time context sensing—can actively reshape educational practices and social norms. Instead of making AI adapt to human variability, we design learning environments and societal protocols that train humans to interact effectively with a fixed, non-responsive AI tutor or system. The study examines how such AI-dictated pedagogical regimes force the development of new human skills (like precise self-assessment and structured communication) and institutional forms, making the AI's limitation a catalyst for specific, directed societal change. This explores a future where AI's technical specifications, not just its outputs, become primary levers of cultural and cognitive evolution.

ideas.title: Use adaptive testing from psychometrics
ideas.description: Incorporate item response theory to dynamically adjust test difficulty based on student performance outdoors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Criteria, Strategies and Research Issues of Context-Aware Ubiquitous Learning

ideas.title: A Triage and Adversarial Validation Framework for High-Stakes Smart Learning Research
ideas.description: This research establishes a systematic method to identify and prioritize the most critical, high-risk issues in smart learning, moving beyond a generic list of topics. It employs an evidence-based triage protocol to rank research gaps by their potential for societal harm and the scarcity of existing mitigating knowledge. The core of the approach is a mandatory adversarial validation phase, where proposed solutions or investigations are rigorously stress-tested against failure modes like bias amplification, privacy breaches, and pedagogical breakdowns before full-scale research proceeds. This ensures resources are focused on preemptively addressing the most severe vulnerabilities in AI-enhanced educational systems.

ideas.title: Adapting Surgical Augmented Reality Frameworks for Smart Learning Facilitation
ideas.description: This research seed expands by drawing an analogy from Computer-Assisted Surgery and Medical Augmented Reality. In that field, a key solution for complex procedures is multi-modal registration and context-aware visualization, where pre-operative 3D scans are registered to the live surgical field to overlay critical structures only when and where they are relevant, guided by surgical step and surgeon gaze to reduce cognitive load. Adapting this solution, the research will investigate an AR system for smart learning facilitation that registers a dynamic model of the learning objective and the learner's real-time activity and cognitive state. The system would then employ context-aware rules to overlay only the most pertinent instructional scaffolds, hints, or resource links directly into the learner's physical environment, aligned with their immediate task and gaze point. This aims to create a 'smart' facilitator that provides expert, just-in-time support while minimizing distraction and cognitive overload, fundamentally adapting a precision guidance paradigm from surgery to education.

ideas.title: Apply design-based research from engineering to TPACK PD
ideas.description: Use iterative design-based research methods to rapidly prototype and refine TPACK professional development programmes.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Review of Technological Pedagogical Content Knowledge

ideas.title: Participatory Co-Design of Ultra-Low-Cost, Phone-Centric Smart Learning Kits
ideas.description: This research direction combines participatory design (PD) from HCI with the development of minimalist smart learning kits. It involves teachers and students in resource-constrained environments as co-designers throughout the entire process, from ideation to prototyping, specifically for kits built around basic mobile phones. This approach ensures the final learning tools and activities are pedagogically sound, contextually appropriate, and maximize the limited technological affordances. The synergy transforms PD from a general design principle into a critical, constraint-driven innovation process for ultra-low-cost educational technology.

ideas.title: Reform alignment is not the primary course goal

ideas.title: A Teacher's Mission Control: Dashboard for Blended Learning Orchestration
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from Air Traffic Control (ATC). The analogous problem is managing multiple, simultaneous, interdependent processes (aircraft) in a dynamic, high-stakes environment where the controller orchestrates rather than directly operates each unit. The solution is an integrated situational display providing a 'common operational picture.' Adapted to education, we propose developing a smart environment dashboard for teachers that functions as a 'Mission Control' for blended learning. It would fuse data from digital learning platforms and physical classroom IoT sensors to create a unified, real-time visualization of all student groups and activities. This empowers the teacher to orchestrate effectively by providing awareness of the entire learning ecosystem, enabling proactive management of transitions, resource allocation, and interventions between online and real-world tasks, scaling their capacity to guide complex, concurrent learning flows.

ideas.title: Apply social network analysis metrics
ideas.description: Use SNA metrics like centrality and density to quantitatively analyze student interaction patterns in educational networks.

ideas.title: Robust Context-Aware Learning Systems via Probabilistic Sensor Fusion
ideas.description: This research develops a novel framework for educational technology that robustly infers a learner's physical and social context by applying advanced sensor fusion techniques from robotics, such as Kalman filters or particle filters, to multi-modal data streams from device sensors, interaction logs, and ambient inputs. The core innovation is the probabilistic modeling of context, which provides a continuous, confidence-weighted estimate of the learner's situation (e.g., '70% likely in a busy cafe, 30% likely on a moving vehicle'). This quantified uncertainty allows the learning system to make meta-cognitive decisions, such as deferring complex instruction during high uncertainty or proactively asking the learner for contextual clarification, thereby creating a dynamically reliable and context-sensitive educational experience impossible with deterministic inference or simple fusion alone.

ideas.description: Investigate if practicing sophisticated search strategies can causally lead to the development of advanced evaluative standards.

ideas.title: Validated Context Co-Inference for Adaptive Learning
ideas.description: This system addresses the ethical and accuracy pitfalls of passive context inference by designing a transparent, participatory process where the learner's environment is co-inferred. It uses learning interactions to generate initial, uncertainty-aware hypotheses about physical or social context (e.g., 'likely in a noisy cafe, 70% confidence'). These hypotheses are then presented through lightweight, non-disruptive interfaces for learner confirmation or correction. The system continuously learns from these validation signals, improving its models while prioritizing learner agency, privacy, and the avoidance of biased assumptions. The result is a robust, ethically-grounded adaptive system that leverages context without imposing it.

ideas.title: Empirical Validation of Smart Learning Systems in Divergent AI Futures
ideas.description: This research synthesizes strategic foresight with empirical validation by first using formal scenario planning to construct 3-4 detailed, divergent scenarios of AI's societal and educational impact over a 10-15 year horizon. Key features and criteria for 'smart learning' systems are then operationalized and tested within experimental or simulated environments modeled on these specific future scenarios. This approach empirically assesses not just if features work in today's settings, but how robust, equitable, and effective they are across fundamentally different possible futures, thereby generating evidence to guide the development of future-proof, adaptive educational technology.

ideas.title: Use computational text analysis on reports
ideas.description: Analyze incident reports or student narratives with NLP to identify hidden patterns and risk factors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Research and trends in science education from 1998 to 2002: a content analysis of publication in selected journals

ideas.title: Cognitive Vaccination: Introducing Information Mismatches to Strengthen Learning
ideas.description: This idea inverts the 'right place, right time' paradigm by exploring if deliberately providing information at the 'wrong' time or place could enhance learning through reflection. Drawing an analogy from Immunology/Vaccinology, where vaccines introduce a weakened pathogen (a 'mismatch') before natural infection to prime a stronger immune response, the adapted solution is a 'cognitive vaccination' approach. The research would design interventions where learners are first exposed to core concepts in a deliberately suboptimal format—such as an incomplete analogy, a puzzle with missing context, or information presented in a semantically 'foreign' environment. This controlled mismatch acts as a 'cognitive antigen,' forcing the brain's learning systems to engage in active reconstruction, error detection, and schema-building. The hypothesis is that this reflective struggle creates more robust and adaptable knowledge structures, making subsequent exposure to the 'right' formal instruction more effective for long-term retention and transfer, much like a vaccine leads to stronger immunity upon later pathogen exposure.

ideas.title: Multi-Granular Adaptation: Dynamically Balancing Group Scaffolding with Individual Safeguards
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid smart learning system that primarily operates at the group or community level to foster collaborative intelligence and reduce profiling overhead. However, it directly addresses the equity flaw of pure group adaptation by incorporating continuous, low-fidelity individual monitoring as a safeguard. The system dynamically clusters learners into fluid sub-groups based on real-time task needs and uses group-level adaptation to guide collaborative problem-solving. If an individual's performance or engagement persistently diverges from their sub-group, the system triggers discrete, supportive interventions—such as tailored hints or optional micro-tasks—to prevent them from being left behind or unchallenged.

ideas.title: Scale up to classroom group eye-tracking
ideas.description: Develop methods to track multiple students' gaze simultaneously in a classroom setting.
ideas.sourceTitle: A review of using eye-tracking technology in exploring learning from 2000 to 2012

ideas.title: Ethnographic Study of Deliberate AI Information Misdirection for Workplace Reflection and Adaptation
ideas.description: This research employs ethnographic methods to study what happens when workplace AI systems are deliberately designed to provide information or prompts at the 'wrong' time or in the 'wrong' context relative to a user's immediate task. By observing real human-AI interactions under these conditions, the study investigates how workers reflect upon, reinterpret, and ultimately adapt both the information and their own workflows. The goal is to develop a framework for 'provocative integration,' where AI acts not merely as an assistant but as a strategic partner in exposing cognitive fixedness and fostering adaptive expertise within complex organizational settings.

ideas.title: Build reciprocal academia-industry collaboration models
ideas.description: Develop formal structures for mutually beneficial partnerships between educational researchers and technology companies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Challenges and Future Directions of Big Data and Artificial Intelligence in Education

ideas.description: Expand the system's capabilities beyond identification tests to include problem-solving or interactive simulations.

ideas.title: Review measurement methods pre-2010 for historical insight
ideas.description: Extend the review period backward to analyze earlier measurement approaches in epistemology.
ideas.sourceTitle: Nested epistemologies: Science teachers' beliefs of teaching, learning and science

ideas.title: Phone-Independent, Peer-to-Peer Mesh Learning Nodes for Off-Grid Education
ideas.description: This system designs dedicated, ultra-low-cost, single-application learning devices that operate completely independently of smartphones and cellular networks. Each rugged, solar-rechargeable node focuses on a core learning task and can form ad-hoc mesh networks with other nearby nodes to sync progress, share content, and enable collaborative activities without central infrastructure. This approach ensures functionality in environments with no connectivity, unstable electricity, or where personal phones are communal or unavailable, making the learning kit robustly accessible in the most constrained settings.

ideas.title: Boundary Design for AI-Resilient Learning Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research investigates how industries that rely on complex, embodied, or socially embedded skills may resist AI disruption by cultivating learning ecosystems with specific 'boundary designs' between digital and physical practice. It moves beyond assuming uniform technological impact to argue that an industry's resilience is partly engineered through pedagogical structures that create productive friction—such as mandatory apprenticeships, tool-specific rituals, or protected reflective spaces—which are difficult to automate or replicate online. The core inquiry examines which boundary designs (e.g., seamless integration vs. strict separation) in professional training correlate with sustained human-centric value and institutional stability in the face of AI advancement.

ideas.title: The Calm Orchestrator: An Attention-Aware Assistant for Blended Classroom Management
ideas.description: This research develops a smart environment that supports teacher orchestration by intelligently managing the flow of information and alerts, preventing overload. The system uses multimodal sensing and explicit teacher input to infer the current classroom activity phase and the teacher's focus of attention. It then applies a triage model to filter notifications from student devices, online platforms, and environment sensors, presenting only prioritized, actionable insights through subtle ambient displays or concise summaries during natural breakpoints. The goal is to reduce cognitive load by making orchestration support context-aware, proactive, and minimally disruptive, thereby enhancing rather than complicating the teacher's supervisory role.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Network Analysis to Model Contradictions in Smart Learning Systems
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Ecology—Ecological Network Analysis (ENA)—to address the challenge of analyzing contradictions and dynamics in smart learning activity systems, as framed by Activity Theory. In ecology, ENA is used to model complex ecosystems by quantifying material/energy flows between components to identify critical nodes, pathways, and systemic imbalances. The adapted idea transposes this methodological approach: the nodes of an activity system (subject, tools, rules, community, division of labor, object) are treated as an interconnected network. The flows between them (e.g., information from tools to subject, artifacts through division of labor, outcomes toward the object) are quantified using data from smart learning environments (log data, interaction traces, artifact versions). By applying network metrics akin to ecological ascendency or cycling indices, researchers can move beyond qualitative identification of contradictions to a computational, model-based analysis that locates flow bottlenecks, inefficient pathways, and unstable configurations, thereby predicting how contradictions dynamically affect the entire learning system's development and outcomes.

ideas.title: Scale down to intensive single-case studies
ideas.description: Use deep phenotyping on individual adolescents to understand unique addiction pathways.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sensation seeking and internet dependence of Taiwanese high school adolescents

ideas.title: Relax the need for embedded, automated data collection
ideas.description: Design effective AI-ed systems that prioritize minimal, student-controlled data input over pervasive tracking.
ideas.sourceTitle: Challenges and Future Directions of Big Data and Artificial Intelligence in Education

ideas.title: A City as a Distributed Cognitive System: Applying Metabolic Network Principles to Urban Learning Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of city-wide learning ecosystems by drawing an analogy from systems biology. The analogous problem is understanding how a cell's metabolic network functions as a coordinated, adaptive whole, solved by mapping metabolites/reactions and analyzing flux. Adapted to the urban learning context, the research would design smart learning environments at a city scale by modeling the integrated system of museums, parks, and transport as a distributed cognitive network. The goal is to map all learning resources and activities, identify key hubs and bottlenecks, and apply principles of network flux to engineer the ecosystem for optimal, resilient, and equitable learning 'metabolism' throughout the city.

ideas.title: Apply Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) from clinical psychology to AI workplace ethnography
ideas.description: This research adapts the Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) method from clinical psychology to the ethnographic study of AI workplace integration. The analogous problem in clinical psychology is understanding how behaviors, symptoms, and cognitions fluctuate in real-world contexts, which was solved by using mobile technology to prompt participants for brief, repeated reports in their natural environments. The adapted idea involves equipping workers with a smartphone application that prompts them multiple times daily to report on their recent AI interactions, task adaptations, cognitive load, and emotional state. This method captures the immediate, context-rich, and dynamic process of human-AI task co-evolution, generating a high-resolution temporal dataset that complements broader ethnographic narratives and reveals patterns invisible to periodic observation alone.

ideas.title: Equitable Access and Data Sovereignty in City-Scale Public Learning Infrastructures
ideas.description: This research designs and prototypes a city-wide learning ecosystem architected as a public utility, prioritizing universal access and data sovereignty. The core is a publicly governed digital commons that maps and integrates learning assets (museums, parks, libraries) into open, interoperable pathways. Access is provided through both digital platforms and guaranteed low-tech channels (e.g., community center terminals, printed resource maps, SMS services) to bridge the digital divide. A foundational governance and technical framework enforces strict data anonymization, prohibits commercial surveillance, and gives learners control over their personal learning records, ensuring the ecosystem serves the public good equitably.

ideas.title: Architecting Smart Learning Systems with a Negotiable and Auditable Utility-Privacy Budget
ideas.description: This research designs a core architectural framework for smart learning systems that formally quantifies and manages the inherent trade-off between data utility and individual privacy. Instead of applying abstract principles, the system integrates a configurable 'Utility-Privacy Budget' mechanism, employing techniques like differential privacy and federated learning as first-class architectural elements. This allows educators and system administrators to explicitly set and dynamically adjust the operational balance based on context, from highly private but less adaptive modes to more data-intensive, personalized learning. The architecture ensures this balance is technically enforced, continuously auditable, and transparent to all stakeholders, transforming a philosophical tension into a manageable engineering parameter.

ideas.title: Apply privacy-by-design from data protection
ideas.description: Integrate privacy-by-design principles from data protection law into the core architecture of smart learning systems.
ideas.sourceTitle: Research trends in mobile and ubiquitous learning: a review of publications in selected journals from 2001 to 2010

ideas.title: Automate the user analysis and prototyping phases
ideas.description: Develop tools that partially automate user task analysis and prototype generation.

ideas.title: Identifying AI's 'Niche Construction' Failures: When Human Tasks Resist Automation
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from evolutionary ecology, specifically the concept of 'niche construction.' In ecology, some species avoid being outcompeted by actively modifying their environment in ways that sustain their niche. The analogous solution involves analyzing these specific feedback mechanisms. Adapted to AI research, this approach shifts the focus from static task analysis to a dynamic model where human performers are not passive but actively construct the value parameters of their tasks. The research would investigate scenarios where humans embed tasks within complex webs of social trust, ethical judgment, tacit contextual understanding, or co-created meaning—modifications that alter the very definition of 'successful' task completion and create barriers to pure AI substitution. By cataloging these 'human-niche construction' mechanisms across fields like healthcare, creative arts, leadership, and skilled trades, the study aims to systematically challenge the assumption of inevitable AI replacement by identifying the dynamic, self-reinforcing human advantages that persist.

ideas.title: Apply Safety-by-Design from Pharmaceutical Manufacturing to Smart Learning Systems
ideas.description: This idea proposes to integrate privacy-by-design principles into smart learning systems by drawing a direct analogy from the pharmaceutical field's rigorous Quality-by-Design (QbD) paradigm. Just as QbD mandates building safety and efficacy into drug manufacturing from the start through defined attributes, parameter control, and a validated design space, we adapt this to smart learning. The adapted research idea is to develop a formal 'Privacy/Safety-by-Design' framework where Critical Privacy/Safety Attributes (CPAs/CSAs) are established for a learning system. The architectural and algorithmic choices (Critical Design Parameters) that influence these attributes are then mapped and controlled within a predefined 'operational design space.' This transforms privacy and safety from post-hoc audits into proactive, engineered, and continuously assured properties of the system's core methodology, ensuring they are as integral to its function as learning accuracy.

ideas.title: Co-Designing Human-AI Work Systems: An Interventional Ethnography Framework
ideas.description: This research establishes a framework of interventional ethnography to move beyond passively studying AI integration to actively shaping it. It combines deep, longitudinal field observation with rapid, participatory design cycles where researchers and workers collaboratively prototype and test AI tools and revised workflows in situ. The continuous ethnographic analysis of these interventions generates real-time, causal insights into what drives successful task redefinition and collaboration. The outcome is a dynamic, evidence-based model for the co-evolution of AI systems and organizational practices that is robust, scalable, and ethically attuned to worker agency.

ideas.title: Modular, Phone-Powered Learning Kits Using Minimalist Protocol Design
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from Low-Power Wide-Area Networks (LPWAN) for IoT, where the problem of connecting many cheap, low-power devices was solved using minimalist protocol design (e.g., LoRaWAN). The analogous solution uses ultra-simple, duty-cycled communication to passive endpoints, with a centralized gateway handling complexity. Adapted to the context of minimalist smart learning kits for resource-constrained environments, the idea is to design learning kits as ultra-simple, passive sensor or input modules (for science, math, or coding) that communicate via basic, robust methods like audio tones, light pulses, or NFC to a central 'gateway'—the student's basic mobile phone. The phone's app handles all complex processing, user interface, and instructional feedback. This architecture allows the physical kit components to be manufactured at ultra-low cost from cardboard, basic circuits, or recycled materials, scaling access by leveraging the existing computational and connectivity resource of the mobile phone as the intelligent hub.

ideas.title: Assessing AI's Impact on Workforce Adaptation at the Team and Community Level
ideas.description: This research investigates the realistic impact of AI in key industries (finance, healthcare, manufacturing, logistics) by focusing on how workforces adapt collectively rather than individually. It moves beyond assessing task automation to model and measure the 'smart learning' benefits when teams, departments, or entire professional communities co-evolve with AI systems, developing shared practices and protocols. The core question is how industry disruption is mediated by the capacity for group-level adaptation, identifying which organizational structures and community knowledge networks most effectively harness AI's potential. This provides a more holistic and actionable assessment of AI's true impact on industry resilience and transformation.

ideas.title: Strategic Temporal Mismatch as a Source of Industry Resilience to AI Disruption
ideas.description: This research investigates whether industries less susceptible to AI disruption are those that systematically leverage 'temporal mismatches'—deliberately providing critical information at the 'wrong' time or place for immediate use. It posits that such practices force deep cognitive reflection, fostering tacit knowledge, strategic foresight, and adaptive problem-solving skills that are difficult for current AI to replicate or automate. The study would identify and analyze resilient industries to see if they institutionalize these mismatches (e.g., through strategic retreats, delayed feedback cycles, or cross-contextual training) as a core competitive mechanism. The goal is to develop a model for how organizations can design information flow to build human cognitive buffers against AI-driven homogenization and real-time optimization.

ideas.title: Participatory Design of Public Sector AI: Co-Creating Governance Systems with Citizens and Civil Servants
ideas.description: This research applies participatory design (PD) methods from HCI to the development and implementation of AI systems within government and the public sector. It moves beyond examining adoption challenges to actively involving citizens, frontline civil servants, and community stakeholders in the co-creation of AI tools for services like benefits adjudication, urban planning, or public health. The synergy transforms PD from a tool for user-centered educational technology into a framework for democratic AI governance, ensuring systems are accountable, equitable, and aligned with public values. This approach aims to build public trust and mitigate risks of bias or alienation that arise from purely technocratic AI solutions.

ideas.title: Privacy-Preserving Human-AI Synergy in High-Stakes Learning Environments
ideas.description: This research investigates how embedding privacy-by-design principles into smart learning systems can create architectural constraints that actively preserve and highlight tasks where human involvement is irreplaceable. It moves beyond data minimization to explore how technical design choices—such as data localization, purpose limitation, and user-centric control—can protect the nuanced, contextual, and trust-based interpersonal interactions essential in domains like therapy, diplomacy, or complex mentorship. The synergy produces a novel design philosophy where privacy engineering does not merely comply with regulation but actively shapes AI systems to augment rather than replace human judgment in high-stakes scenarios, creating a new benchmark for human-AI collaboration.

ideas.title: Inverse Context Inference via Seismic-Style Signal Deconvolution
ideas.description: This research adapts the core inverse problem from geophysics—inferring hidden subsurface structures from surface seismic waves—to the challenge of inferring a learner's context from their interaction data. In seismology, deconvolution and waveform inversion techniques are used to estimate geological layers from recorded signals. Here, we develop analogous 'educational seismology' systems. A learner's interactions (keystrokes, mouse movements, response times, error patterns) are treated as a composite signal. Machine learning models, structured like seismic inversion algorithms, are trained to deconvolve this signal: first removing the expected 'system impulse response' (the baseline interface design and content difficulty), then analyzing the residual waveform to classify the probable external context. This could distinguish between a quiet home, a mobile commute, or a supervised classroom, and between individual or collaborative social modes, enabling the learning system to dynamically adapt its scaffolding and feedback based on this inferred, hidden state.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Impact Assessment Frameworks to Forecast AI's Industry-Specific Disruption
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodological framework of Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) from ecology to the problem of forecasting AI's disruptive impact across specific industries. In ecology, EIAs provide a structured process to predict how a new intervention (e.g., introducing a species or building infrastructure) will affect a complex ecosystem's components and functions. The adapted approach will treat industries like finance, healthcare, manufacturing, and logistics as distinct socio-technical ecosystems. For each, researchers will first map the ecosystem's key 'species' (job roles, legacy systems, regulatory bodies, capital flows) and 'food webs' (workflow interdependencies). Then, specific AI capabilities (e.g., generative models, reinforcement learning) will be analyzed as the intervening force. The assessment will model direct effects (automation of specific tasks), indirect secondary effects (emergence of new service models, shifts in required skills), and cumulative risks (increased systemic fragility, ethical externalities). The output will be a comparative, scenario-based impact forecast for each industry, moving beyond generic speculation to provide a structured, evidence-informed assessment tool for policymakers and industry leaders.

ideas.title: Modeling Immutable Constraints: A Framework for Predicting AI-Resistant Human Tasks
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a theoretical framework identifying fundamental socio-technical constraints that prevent AI from replacing specific human tasks, regardless of improvements in capability. It moves beyond anecdotal failure cases to model causal mechanisms—such as the need for performative legitimacy, deeply contextual value negotiation, or embodied situational awareness—that are irreducible to information processing. The framework is tested through computational simulations of task displacement under hypothetical AI advances and longitudinal analysis of domains previously predicted to automate. The outcome is a predictive model for identifying durable human-AI complementarity in organizational and social systems.

ideas.title: Quantifying Bidirectional Causal Loops Between AI Capability Shocks and Societal Adaptation
ideas.description: This research develops a computational framework to model and measure the dynamic, two-way influence between AI development and societal change. It moves beyond a simple directional inversion to formalize specific feedback mechanisms—such as how labor market shocks from AI alter educational policy, which in turn shapes the demand for future AI skills. The goal is to identify leverage points where proactive policy, design choices, or institutional innovation can steer this co-evolution toward more desirable outcomes, transforming observation into a tool for guided adaptation.

ideas.title: Predictive Sensor Fusion for AI Adoption Readiness Assessment
ideas.description: This research direction proposes applying sensor fusion techniques from robotics—such as Kalman filters or Bayesian networks—not to physical sensors, but to the diverse, often noisy, digital data streams within an organization (e.g., process logs, workforce skill data, IT infrastructure metrics, market signals). The fused output generates a high-fidelity, multi-dimensional 'organizational context signal.' This signal is then used as the primary input for technology adoption lifecycle models (like Diffusion of Innovations) to move beyond generic sectoral predictions. The result is a dynamic, data-driven framework that can diagnose an entity's specific position on the AI adoption curve, identify critical fusion points or 'sensor failures' (e.g., data silos, skill gaps) that act as adoption barriers, and prescribe targeted interventions to accelerate integration.

ideas.title: City-Scale Situational Learning via Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion
ideas.description: This research proposes the design of a city-wide learning ecosystem powered by robust sensor fusion techniques from robotics. By integrating data from distributed, heterogeneous sensors across museums, parks, transport, and public infrastructure—such as environmental sensors, public Wi-Fi, anonymized camera feeds, and personal device signals—the system uses probabilistic filtering and deep learning fusion models to create a high-fidelity, real-time map of learning opportunities and engagements. This allows the urban environment to dynamically curate and recommend learning pathways, adjust interactive exhibits, or provide contextual information precisely tailored to the detected interests and collective learning patterns of its citizens, transforming the noisy city into an intelligently responsive educational organism.

ideas.title: Online Self-Calibrating Meta-Fusion for Robust Context Awareness in Heterogeneous Sensor Environments
ideas.description: This system addresses the fragility of static sensor fusion by implementing a two-tier architecture where a lower level performs traditional fusion, while an upper meta-fusion layer continuously estimates the accuracy and bias of each sensor and fusion pathway. It employs online learning to build dynamic reliability models, enabling the system to detect sensor degradation, adversarial spoofing, or environmental interference. The approach cross-validates context hypotheses across modalities, dynamically reweighting or excluding unreliable sensors to maintain robust performance in unpredictable real-world settings.

ideas.title: Sociotechnical Governance of AI Pace via Embedded Privacy Architectures
ideas.description: This research investigates how the deliberate architectural integration of privacy-by-design principles into smart learning systems acts as a key societal variable influencing the pace and trajectory of AI development. It moves beyond treating privacy as a mere legal requirement, instead modeling it as a core technical constraint that shapes R&D investment decisions, competitive dynamics, and public acceptance. The novel synergy lies in using this embedded technical-legal artifact as a measurable lens to study the feedback loops between industrial development speed, regulatory environments, and societal values, ultimately proposing mechanisms to align AI progress with democratically chosen constraints like privacy.

ideas.title: The Embedded Observatory: A Dynamic Framework for Real-Time Research on AI Emergence
ideas.description: This project establishes a permanent, embedded research infrastructure—an observatory—within the AI development ecosystem. Instead of a static agenda, it deploys automated sensors to collect real-time data on key indicators of emergence (e.g., capability benchmarks, compute usage, model releases). This data stream feeds into a suite of pre-defined, adaptive research protocols that activate in response to specific thresholds or unexpected events, generating timely studies on opportunities and risks. The system is designed for perpetual self-update, ensuring the research remains synchronized with the actual pace of technological change.

ideas.title: Applying Epidemiological Compartmental Models to AI Adoption Dynamics
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in epidemiology—modeling disease spread—to the context of AI adoption. In epidemiology, compartmental models like SIR (Susceptible, Infected, Recovered) successfully predict the diffusion dynamics of a pathogen through a population by modeling state transitions with rate parameters. The analogous solution is adapted by treating AI technology as the 'agent' spreading through a sector's population of potential adopters. The research would define analogous compartments (e.g., S: unaware/non-adopters, I: evaluators and implementers, R: full operational users), and parameterize the model with rates derived from sector-specific factors like network effects, implementation costs, and regulatory hurdles. This approach moves beyond static adoption lifecycle curves, enabling dynamic, testable simulations of how AI adoption might surge, stall, or reach equilibrium within different industries, providing a quantitative tool for policymakers and strategists.

ideas.title: Causal Analysis of Societal Pressure on Critical AI Development Decisions
ideas.description: This research employs a comparative, quasi-experimental design to establish causal links between societal factors and specific, high-consequence AI development choices. It identifies matched pairs of similar AI organizations or projects operating under different societal pressures (e.g., regulatory environments, public sentiment, competitive landscapes) and uses process-tracing to analyze their divergent decisions at key junctures. The study tests falsifiable hypotheses about the mechanisms of influence, moving beyond correlation to identify which societal levers most effectively alter development pace and direction. The output is a causal model that informs targeted governance and corporate strategy.

ideas.title: A Research Agenda for AI's Rapid Emergence, Modeled on Synthetic Biology Governance
ideas.description: This research agenda is constructed by analogy to the governance challenges posed by the rapid emergence of synthetic biology. The analogous solution involved creating interdisciplinary frameworks for anticipatory governance, focusing on real-time risk assessment, safety-by-design, and adaptive policy. Adapted for AI, the agenda proposes a core research pillar dedicated to 'Anticipatory Governance for Rapid AI Capability Emergence.' Key research threads would include: 1) Mapping the 'AI capability ecology' to forecast trajectories and interdependencies; 2) Developing embedded technical safeguards (e.g., scalable oversight tools, containment protocols) as integral components of advanced AI systems; 3) Designing dynamic regulatory and institutional models that can test and adapt to novel AI impacts in real-time; and 4) Establishing continuous, interdisciplinary technology assessment processes to identify dual-use risks, economic disruptions, and societal impacts proactively, not reactively. The goal is to build a research infrastructure that keeps pace with AI development itself.

ideas.title: Co-Designing and Validating Public Trust Stress Tests for High-Stakes Government AI Systems
ideas.description: This research moves beyond cataloging challenges to directly address the core crisis of legitimacy in public sector AI. It develops a rigorous methodology for creating 'Public Trust Stress Tests'—specific audit procedures designed to probe algorithmic systems for fairness, explainability, and bias—through participatory workshops with citizens, policymakers, and technical experts. These co-designed tests are then applied and validated in case studies of real or proposed AI deployments in areas like benefits distribution, predictive policing, and resource allocation. The output is a tested, open framework for actionable audit protocols that agencies can adopt to build demonstrable accountability and preemptively address the socio-technical flaws that undermine public trust.

ideas.title: Modeling AI as a Geological Force: Applying Earth System Science to Societal Co-evolution
ideas.description: This research seed inverts the direction of societal influence on AI by drawing an analogy from Earth System Science. The analogous problem was recognizing human activity as a geological force defining the Anthropocene epoch, which was solved by developing coupled Earth-human system models and identifying formal stratigraphic markers of human impact. Adapting this solution, the research proposes to treat AI's rapid development as an autonomous, systemic force that dictates societal change. It would develop novel coupled models that integrate AI's intrinsic capability trajectories (e.g., scaling laws, emergent behaviors) with models of socioeconomic and political systems to simulate co-evolution. The aim is to identify and define measurable, irreversible 'AI stratigraphic markers'—such as specific displacements in labor economics, transformations in political communication, or shifts in scientific discovery rates—to formally characterize and anticipate how AI, as a force, is reshaping the foundational strata of society.

ideas.title: Adapting Clinical Trial Protocols for the Phased, Auditable Deployment of Public Sector AI
ideas.description: This research seed expands the exploration of AI in government by drawing an analogy from clinical pharmacology. The analogous problem—introducing a new, potent, and potentially risky intervention into a complex human system—was solved through structured clinical trial protocols. We adapt this solution to the public sector context. The research will examine how a formal, multi-phase deployment protocol, inspired by clinical trials (Phase I-IV), can be designed to manage the risks of AI adoption in government services. This involves defining stages from limited proof-of-concept to broad rollout, each with specific safety, efficacy, and equity evaluation criteria, independent audit mechanisms, and a transparent registry for system performance and incidents. The goal is to develop a governance framework that balances innovation with public accountability, trust, and rigorous oversight, directly addressing the core challenge of responsible AI adoption in the public domain.

ideas.title: Adapting Climate Model Ensembles for AI Scenario Planning
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from climate science to the methodological challenge of exploring AI's divergent societal futures. In climate science, projections are made using ensembles of multiple global climate models (GCMs) under shared emission scenarios to quantify uncertainty and identify robust trends. The analogous solution is to apply this ensemble modeling philosophy to formal scenario planning for AI. Instead of relying on a single scenario planning method, the research would construct a methodological ensemble—integrating distinct frameworks like Intuitive Logics, Cross-Impact Analysis, and System Dynamics models—all focused on the same core drivers of AI impact. This adapted approach systematically generates and compares scenario families from each method, allowing researchers to quantify uncertainty ranges for key outcomes, identify future states that are robust across methodological choices, and ultimately develop more resilient and methodologically rigorous meta-scenarios for strategic decision-making regarding AI's societal trajectory.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Resilience Theory to AI Industry Disruption
ideas.description: This research adapts ecological resilience theory to challenge the assumption of uniform AI disruption across industries. Drawing from ecology—where scientists analyze why some ecosystems withstand disturbances through properties like functional redundancy, modularity, and response diversity—we propose mapping similar resilience properties to industrial structures. The adapted research idea examines industries as complex systems, identifying characteristics that buffer against AI disruption: functional redundancy (multiple non-AI dependent processes that maintain core functions), modularity (compartmentalized workflows that contain disruption), and response diversity (varied adaptation strategies among firms within an industry). This approach creates a predictive framework for identifying resilient industries and understanding the systemic properties that mediate technological disruption.

ideas.title: Applying Innovation Ecosystem Analysis from Pharmaceutical R&D to AI Development
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from pharmaceutical R&D ecosystem analysis to investigate how societal and industrial factors influence AI development pace. In pharmaceuticals, system dynamics and multi-agent models successfully simulate how regulatory environments, market forces, public funding, and societal pressures collectively steer the speed and therapeutic focus of innovation. The adapted idea involves building a similar computational model for the AI innovation ecosystem, parameterizing factors like regulatory landscapes, public and private investment patterns, competitive corporate dynamics, talent flows, and the intensity of public ethical discourse. By simulating different societal and industrial scenarios, this model would aim to predict their causal impact on the overall velocity and prioritization of key research directions within AI development, offering a systems-level view of technological trajectory.

ideas.title: Simulate AI's Disruptive Pathways: A Dynamic System Model for Finance, Healthcare, Manufacturing, and Logistics
ideas.description: This research moves beyond static assessment to build and calibrate a suite of industry-specific computational models that simulate the non-linear adoption and impact of AI technologies. It incorporates agent-based modeling and system dynamics to represent key stakeholders (firms, regulators, workers) and critical constraints (data availability, infrastructure, safety standards). The output is a set of conditional, quantified impact scenarios with identified leverage points and potential policy interventions, providing a robust tool for strategic planning under uncertainty.

ideas.title: Agent-Based Scenario Simulation for Stress-Testing AI Governance Strategies
ideas.description: This research integrates formal scenario planning with multi-agent computational modeling to overcome the abstraction and inertia of traditional futures work. It translates high-level scenario narratives into parameterized simulation environments where autonomous agents, endowed with different goals and behavioral rules, interact. By running these agent-based models across multiple, divergent scenarios, the system generates concrete, emergent data on potential policy outcomes, regulatory failures, and strategic risks. This allows for the rigorous stress-testing of specific AI governance proposals and organizational strategies against a wide range of plausible futures.

ideas.title: Adopt Bayesian Belief Updating from Cognitive Neuroscience for Context-Aware Sensor Fusion
ideas.description: This idea expands the original sensor fusion concept by drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience. The analogous problem is how the human brain seamlessly integrates unreliable and conflicting sensory streams to form a coherent perception of the environment, solved through hierarchical Bayesian belief updating as described in predictive coding theories. The adapted research idea is to implement a sensor fusion algorithm for context detection that explicitly models this neural process. The system will maintain a probabilistic belief state about context, dynamically updating it by weighting inputs from multiple noisy sensors (audio, motion, location) according to their estimated precision and prior contextual probabilities. This biologically-inspired, Bayesian approach aims to significantly improve the accuracy and robustness of context detection in challenging real-world settings by mimicking the brain's solution to the same fundamental problem of integrating uncertain multi-modal data.
